Its Norwegian presence and brand are directly competitive with Solvera's initial market. However, Rambøll's computational MEP design tools (Rhino, Grasshopper, Revit Dynamo, MagiCAD) remain human-operated parametric tools — not agentic, physics-based multi-variable optimizers. Rambøll uses AI for computational design and advanced analytics but has not disclosed any AI-native MEP automation capability that eliminates human design hours. Its foundation ownership structure and employee-partner model create deep-rooted resistance to fixed-rate or outcome-based pricing transformation. FY2025 was described by its own CEO as 'unsatisfactory,' with revenue and earnings below expectations, suggesting margin pressure that could make it slower to invest in disruptive internal product development.","techStack":"Autodesk Revit (primary BIM platform), MagiCAD (MEP engineering in Revit), Rhino + Grasshopper (parametric and generative MEP design), Revit Dynamo (workflow automation and visual programming), computational simulation tools (thermal, CFD, acoustic); proprietary benchmark databases for tall building structural and MEP performance; advanced analytics and parametric optimization pipelines (Python-based scripting likely); digital twin capabilities (building performance modeling); international expansion to US, UK, Germany planned post-seed; targeting MEP consulting firms (B2B SaaS) rather than end clients directly — i.e., selling to the engineers, not competing with them","differentiation":"Endra is the most proximate startup threat to Solvera by problem domain — both target MEP design automation using AI. Solvera proposes to be the engineering firm, bearing the professional stamp and liability directly. Endra's current scope is electrical network design only (lighting, power distribution, fire alarms), with HVAC and plumbing on a 12-month roadmap — Solvera's ceiling space optimization wedge directly overlaps HVAC/MEP routing in the ceiling void, making this a future collision course. Endra's stated moat is its proprietary geometry engine and 3D data model; Solvera's proposed moat is compounding institutional knowledge from stamped project outcomes. Incumbent firms (Sweco, Rambøll, AFRY, AECOM) operate on hourly billing where inefficiency is revenue, trapping 50% of engineer time in manual, repetitive coordination. A structural workforce shortage across Europe—over 30,000 unfilled MEP specialist roles in Europe alone—sits on the critical path to doubling construction output by 2040. Agentic AI now makes it possible to break the 1:1 leverage on human capital for the first time. Anthropic's Economic Index confirms architecture and engineering has 84.8% AI automation potential but only ~5% observed adoption—the widest capability-adoption gap of any professional sector—creating a rare, durable arbitrage window for an AI-native entrant.","companyOverview":"Solvera is an AI-native MEP engineering firm that owns the full stack: proprietary technology (geometry kernel, physics-based multi-variable optimization engine, agentic workflow automation) plus licensed engineers on staff who carry legal liability and stamp deliverables. It sells directly to contractors and owners—displacing incumbents rather than selling into them—starting with ceiling space optimization (MEP clash resolution and system routing) for commercial and multi-family residential projects in Norway. The operating model is designed to compound institutional engineering knowledge across projects via a codified data flywheel, decoupling revenue growth from headcount growth and structurally targeting 50–60% gross margins within 12–18 months versus the 30–35% gross margin standard at traditional firms.","willingnessToPay":"• Speed premium: ceiling space coordination today takes weeks-to-months of iterative clash-detection; Solvera collapses this to hours-to-days, directly reducing project schedule risk and financing carry costs for contractors\n• Fixed-price certainty: contractors and owners pay 1–2% of construction cost for MEP design; Solvera offers fixed competitive rates vs. time-and-materials incumbents, removing cost overrun risk—a persistent pain point cited in BIM coordination research showing $5–$15 returned per $1 invested in coordination\n• Quality uplift: incumbents find the 'first answer that doesn't crash' rather than an optimized one; Solvera's physics-based optimizer explicitly targets material cost reduction, energy efficiency, and constructability simultaneously\n• Workforce scarcity hedge: with 30,000+ unfilled MEP specialist roles in Europe, contractors face service unavailability from incumbents; Solvera provides an alternative supply of stamped deliverables\n• Regulatory compliance tailwind: EU EPBD mandates zero-emission new buildings by 2030, requiring more complex MEP design for heat pumps, electrification, and smart controls—work for which incumbent capacity is structurally insufficient\n• Consigly gap opportunity: AECOM's acquisition and subsequent contract pullout from Consigly has left Norwegian contractors—including Bethelmust—without their previous modern tooling, creating an immediate, warm demand signal\n• Data flywheel lock-in: as Solvera codifies judgment calls per project, switching costs rise and output quality compounds, supporting price stability and customer retention","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Fixed-price per-project contracts (lump sum), scoped per building and phase. Pricing undercuts incumbents on a per-deliverable basis while targeting 50–60% gross margin vs. incumbents' 30–35%, enabled by AI-augmented engineer leverage. No SaaS seat fees; revenue is project-based and recurring across a contractor's project portfolio. Strategic optionality exists to layer a platform/software licensing model if engineer-to-project ratios fall sufficiently to warrant a model pivot.","estimatedAcv":250000,"unitName":"mep design projects","tamUnits":80000,"samUnits":12000,"somUnits":150},"methodology":"TAM: Anchored to the global MEP design & engineering consulting sub-segment (~$56B per Technavio 2024 C&E segment; consistent with Solvera's $130B total MEP framing net of installation/maintenance). TAM = 80,000 projects × $250K = $20B, narrowed to design services only (excludes installation and maintenance), consistent with the $15B Europe framing in the deck. SAM: Europe-focused geographic scope covering Norway (~1,000 relevant buildings/year per founder memo), UK (~5,000 commercial/multifamily starts requiring MEP design firms, per UK construction output data), and France (~4,000 per INSEE construction pipeline), plus adjacent Nordics (Sweden, Denmark ~2,000 combined) = ~12,000 projects/year. SAM = 12,000 × $250K = $3B. SOM: 3–5 year capture in Norway (target: 50–75 projects/year at full Norway run-rate by Year 3, per ~5–8% of 1,000 addressable buildings) plus early UK and France traction (50–75 projects combined across 2 markets). Total SOM = ~150 projects × $250K = ~$37.5M ARR-equivalent run-rate in 3–5 years. The wedge is radical simplicity: one URL input, zero briefs, zero dashboards, deliberate cohort-gated onboarding — positioning Glovi as the first 'marketing co-founder' for the solo builder archetype.","willingnessToPay":"• Cost displacement: Replaces $3,000–$8,000/month fractional marketer or social media agency retainer at a fraction of the cost — a 10–40x ROI signal for cash-constrained founders.\n• Opportunity cost: Solo founders report losing 10–15 strategic hours/week to social presence management; Glovi reclaims that time directly for product development.\n• Distribution is the #1 kill factor: 70% of micro-SaaS products fail due to weak distribution, not weak product — Glovi addresses the single highest-stakes risk in the segment.\n• Zero-overhead workflow: Slack-native delivery eliminates tool fatigue and onboarding friction — indie builders already live in Slack, making adoption near-zero-cost.\n• Continuous self-optimization: Unlike a human hire or static scheduler, Glovi compounds performance over time via closed-loop feedback, increasing LTV for the customer.\n• Stage-appropriate pricing: At $150–$299/month, Glovi sits well below the psychological threshold of 'hiring someone' but above 'throwaway tool' — matching the indie dev budget comfort zone.","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Tiered monthly SaaS subscription (per active product/brand) with a usage-based overage layer on actions (posts, conversation joins, channel scans). Extracted text:\n\n--- Page 1 ---\n\n--- Page 2 ---\n\n--- Page 3 ---\n\n--- Page 4 ---\n\n--- Page 5 ---\n\n--- Page 6 ---\n\n--- Page 7 ---\n\n--- Page 8 ---\n\n--- Page 9 ---\n\n--- Page 10 ---\n\n--- Page 11 ---\n\n--- Page 12 ---\n\n--- Page 13 ---\n\n--- Page 14 ---\n\n--- Page 15 ---\n\n--- Page 16 ---\n\n--- Page 17 ---\n\n","via":"pdf"}],"createdAt":1782203411884,"starred":true,"marketStudy":{"problemThesis":"The internet is undergoing a structural inversion: AI-generated traffic is growing 6.5x faster than human traffic, yet the overwhelming majority of software—built for human GUIs—is invisible and unusable to AI agents. There is no canonical discovery layer for agent-callable services, no standardized protocol for converting legacy software into machine-actionable interfaces, and no infrastructure to translate raw human intent into orchestrated multi-step agent actions. This is the defining infrastructure gap of the agentic era: every business that fails to become agent-callable will be systematically bypassed—analogous to companies that refused to build mobile or cloud interfaces a decade ago. Venture capital conviction is unambiguous, with agentic AI startups raising an estimated $6.5–7B in 2025 alone, yet the foundational plumbing—intent translation, agent discovery, legacy software conversion—remains largely unbuilt. The market window is narrow and accelerating.","companyOverview":"Iris LabX is building the four-layer operating infrastructure of the 'agent web,' targeting software companies as its primary B2B wedge. Agentizer converts legacy and modern SaaS software into agent-callable interfaces via MCP endpoints, CLIs, and computer-use fallback—eliminating the need for bespoke integrations. AgentNet provides a discovery and indexing layer through which AI agents can locate, authenticate, and invoke agent-callable services, functioning as the 'DNS + App Store' of the agent web. LiM (Large Intention Models) sits above the protocol layer, translating ambiguous human intent into structured machine actions with persistent context. Iris is the consumer-facing personal AI agent that orchestrates across all three layers to complete real-world tasks end-to-end. This full-stack, vertically integrated approach—owning the intent layer, the protocol standard, the index, and the end-user agent—creates compounding moats at each layer and positions Iris LabX as potential category-defining infrastructure, analogous to what Twilio did for communications or Stripe for payments.","willingnessToPay":"• Agent-Callable Imperative: Software companies face existential risk of becoming invisible to AI agents; 59% of all AI-driven traffic concentrated in e-commerce, media, and travel—the exact verticals Agentizer targets first.\n• Legacy Integration Elimination: Before MCP, each AI-to-tool connection required custom integration; BCG frames the alternative as 'quadratic complexity growth.' Agentizer removes this cost entirely.\n• Revenue Discovery Risk: Businesses not indexed on AgentNet are structurally excluded from agent-initiated commerce—equivalent to having no presence on Google in 2005.\n• Developer Productivity ROI: MCP-native tools report 50–75% time savings on common enterprise tasks; companies that delay agent-readiness widen competitive gaps exponentially.\n• Outcome-Tied Value: LiM's intent-to-action translation replaces costly human coordination workflows; enterprises report average 171% ROI from agentic deployments, well above traditional automation.\n• Infrastructure Stickiness: Deep integration into API surfaces and workflow orchestration creates high switching costs, supporting durable annual contracts with strong NRR expansion potential.","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Hybrid annual platform subscription + usage-based overage tiers, structured across three revenue streams: (1) Agentizer B2B SaaS license—annual contract per software company for MCP endpoint conversion and maintenance, tiered by number of endpoints and API call volume; (2) AgentNet listing and distribution fee—annual subscription for discovery indexing plus per-successful-agent-invocation transaction fee (analogous to an API marketplace commission); (3) LiM/Iris enterprise orchestration license—annual seat-equivalent contract for enterprise teams deploying Iris as an internal agent orchestration layer, billed per active workflow or per agent-completed task at scale. Early-stage go-to-market is developer-led PLG (free tier with usage caps) converting to enterprise annual contracts of $36K–$120K ACV depending on endpoint volume, workflow complexity, and enterprise governance requirements.","estimatedAcv":60000,"unitName":"software companies","tamUnits":500000,"samUnits":75000,"somUnits":1500},"methodology":"TAM of 500,000 software companies derived from the global population of SaaS vendors, B2B API platforms, legacy enterprise software providers, and digital service operators with meaningful API surfaces that must become agent-callable. The global SaaS market alone exceeds 30,000 named vendors (Fortune Business Insights: $315.68B market in 2025); when extended to include all API-exposed B2B software, commerce platforms, travel systems, and legacy enterprise tools in the US, EU, and major APAC markets, the addressable universe exceeds 500,000 entities. This is supported by the MCP ecosystem's rapid growth—over 15,000 GitHub repositories and 9,600+ official MCP registry entries already exist as leading indicators of demand—and by Fastly's finding that AI traffic is growing 6.5x faster than human traffic, forcing every software operator to reckon with agent-accessibility. SAM of 75,000 narrows to English-language, mid-market to enterprise SaaS and B2B platform companies in the US and EU with active API roadmaps, AI readiness budgets, and at least 10 enterprise customers—the segment most acutely experiencing agent-discovery pressure and able to absorb $36K–$120K annual contracts. This is consistent with Gartner's projection that 40% of enterprise applications will embed task-specific AI agents by end of 2026. SOM of 1,500 reflects a realistic 3–5 year capture rate of ~2% of SAM, achievable via developer-led PLG conversion through the Agentizer free tier, enterprise upsell from AgentNet listing customers, and direct enterprise sales. Benchmarked against comparable infra-layer companies (Twilio, Segment, MCP tooling vendors) at equivalent funding and team stages. ACV of $60,000 is calibrated to infrastructure/DevOps SaaS benchmarks ($50K–$150K per Optifai/OpenView) and reflects a blended rate across developer-tier ($24K) and enterprise-tier ($96K) annual contracts, consistent with the hybrid subscription-plus-usage model dominant among successful agentic AI platforms in 2025–2026."}},{"geo":"US","priority":"Medium","founders":[{"name":"Kirill Solodskih","linkedin":"https://www.linkedin.com/in/kirillsolodskih/"},{"name":"Azim Kurbanov","linkedin":"https://www.linkedin.com/in/azim-kurbanov/"},{"name":"Ruslan Aydarkhanov","linkedin":"https://www.linkedin.com/in/ruslan-aydarkhanov/"},{"name":"Max Petriev","linkedin":"https://www.linkedin.com/in/max-petriev/"}],"assets":[{"name":"TheStageTeaser_03_2026.pdf","url":"/uploads/TheStageTeaser_03_2026_819b28bb.pdf","ts":1782200803732},{"name":"TheStage AI Competitive Analysis (1).pdf","url":"/uploads/TheStageAICompetitiveAnalysis1_20151337.pdf","ts":1782278870805}],"preemption":false,"preemptionModel":null,"id":"i67eqt30","name":"TheStage AI","url":"https://app.thestage.ai/","oneLiner":"Automated AI inference optimization platform (ANNA/QLIP) that cuts GPU deployment costs up to 5x for any hardware","sector":"AI Inference / MLOps","stage":"Seed","source":"Manual","status":"To Kill","overview":{"product":"TheStage AI offers an automated inference acceleration stack anchored by ANNA (Automatic NNs Analyzer), which uses discrete math algorithms and AI to auto-optimize PyTorch models via quantization, pruning, and sparsification—reducing optimization time from months to hours with up to 5x cost reduction. The platform also includes the QLIP framework (train, compress, deploy, serve), Elastic Models (pre-optimized open-source models on a quality/speed slider), and a Model Library spanning cloud GPUs to edge/mobile devices. A newer on-device iOS/Android inference SDK targets NPU execution on smartphones and laptops, recently expanded to support voice agents.","market":"The AI inference optimization software market is a high-growth subsegment of the broader AI inference market, valued at ~$106B in 2025 and projected to reach $255–520B by 2030–2034 at ~19% CAGR. Client-reported 3x acceleration and up to 8x storage/inference cost reduction are claimed figures from company materials—treat as unaudited."},"scores":{"proprietaryTech":4,"dataMoat":3,"distributionSwitching":3,"modelAgnostic":4,"ipTimeToReplicate":4},"rationale":{"proprietaryTech":"ANNA is grounded in peer-reviewed discrete math / algebraic topology research (CVPR 2023 award candidate) with 10+ granted patents, giving it genuine technical differentiation over purely heuristic compression tools.","dataMoat":"Each customer model upload enriches calibration datasets and optimization benchmarks, creating a flywheel, but the moat is still early-stage and not yet clearly defensible against well-resourced competitors like NVIDIA NIM or PyTorch-native tooling.","distributionSwitching":"Integration into existing MLOps pipelines (SageMaker, Azure) and the QLIP framework creates moderate workflow lock-in, though switching costs remain relatively low if a competitor matches performance at lower price.","modelAgnostic":"Platform supports PyTorch models across NVIDIA GPUs, Qualcomm NPU, Apple Silicon, and edge devices with a hardware-agnostic intermediate instruction format, making it broadly applicable regardless of which foundation model wins.","ipTimeToReplicate":"The combination of PhD-level discrete math expertise, 15+ papers, 10+ patents, and years of Huawei production experience would take a well-funded team 2–3 years to replicate meaningfully, providing a credible lead-time moat."},"notes":[{"ts":1782129682577,"text":"raising 10 mil led by AIC with 4.5","via":"text/voice"},{"ts":1782200841734,"text":"Pitch Deck PDF Ingested: TheStageTeaser_03_2026.pdf (14880 KB). Competitors Leave Critical Gaps","content":"MiraiAI and Cactus use handcrafted kernels lacking cross-hardware portability; TheStage AI's automated, hardware-agnostic layer captures what they cannot.","bullets":["Comps: Fireworks, Modal, Baseten (cloud-only)","Rivals block NPU / low-bit inference at edge","Qualcomm acquired Nexa AI: M&A validation"]},"slide5":{"title":"BNVT Value Add: Capital, Network, Global Reach","content":"BNVT bridges TheStage AI to DST/Iconiq/TPG/QED late-stage co-investors, NY enterprise channels, and Stockholm's deep AI infrastructure partner network.","bullets":["Stockholm partners: Nordic AI infra ecosystem access","NY bridge: enterprise and Tier-1 VC introductions","DST/Iconiq/TPG/QED: Series A–B co-investment pipeline"]}},"diligenceMemo":{"problemThesis":"The AI inference cost crisis is structurally acute and worsening at scale. As enterprises have moved from AI experimentation to production deployment, the economics of serving models have emerged as the dominant operational constraint—not model quality or availability. Industry data confirms that inference now accounts for 80–90% of the lifetime compute cost of a production AI system, accruing continuously from the moment a model ships to every API call thereafter. GPU infrastructure—predominantly NVIDIA H100/H200 at $2–6 per GPU-hour—represents the single largest line item in AI operating budgets, with industry analysts estimating 55–80% of enterprise AI GPU spend going to inference workloads. This structural cost burden has created a highly specific, high-urgency pain: AI/ML teams at both startups and enterprises are deploying PyTorch models that are not hardware-optimized, resulting in 2–5x GPU overspend relative to what best-practice quantization, pruning, and sparsification would require. The traditional remediation path—manual model compression by specialist ML engineers—takes months, requires deep expertise at the intersection of discrete mathematics, hardware architecture, and deep learning, and is effectively inaccessible to the vast majority of AI engineering teams who lack that specialization. This is the precise gap TheStage AI targets: an automated, agentic optimization layer—ANNA (Automatic NNs Analyzer)—that replaces the manual, months-long model compression workflow with an AI-driven, hardware-aware analysis and compression pipeline executable in hours. The agentic dimension is critical: ANNA autonomously navigates the multi-dimensional tradeoff space between model accuracy, latency, memory footprint, and hardware utilization without requiring practitioners to understand discrete math or low-level compiler engineering. This positions TheStage AI not as a workflow tool but as an intelligence layer inserted between model training and production deployment—an 'optimization agent' that, as models proliferate across cloud, edge, and on-device targets, becomes structurally embedded in every AI deployment pipeline. The expansion of the product suite to QLIP (train, compress, deploy, serve), Elastic Models, and a mobile NPU SDK further amplifies this thesis: as AI moves from centralized cloud inference to hybrid and on-device inference—with Gartner projecting 65%+ of AI IaaS spend supporting inference by 2029—the demand for hardware-agnostic, automated optimization compounds. The agentic inference optimization layer is not a point solution but a foundational infrastructure bet on where AI deployment economics are heading.","marketSizingCheck":"TheStage AI's cited TAM of ~$106B in 2025 growing to $255–520B by 2030–2034 at ~19% CAGR is well-corroborated by multiple independent tier-1 research providers and is, if anything, conservative relative to the broadest definitions. MarketsandMarkets (MnM) pegs the global AI Inference market at exactly $106.15B in 2025, projecting $254.98B by 2030 at a 19.2% CAGR—a near-perfect match to the company's stated figures. Grand View Research estimates $97.24B in 2024 growing to $253.75B by 2030 at 17.5% CAGR, corroborating the trajectory from an adjacent baseline. Polaris Market Research values the 2025 market at $106.0B with a $520.7B projection by 2034 at 19.4% CAGR—the upper bound cited in TheStage AI's materials. Fortune Business Insights confirms North America alone contributed $43.34B (41.78% of global) in 2025. Important definitional note: these TAM figures encompass the entire AI inference stack—hardware (GPUs, ASICs, FPGAs), memory (HBM), networking, and software—with hardware representing the dominant segment (~52% of revenue via GPU segment per Grand View). TheStage AI's actual SAM is the AI inference software and optimization layer (MLOps, model compression, inference frameworks), which is more conservatively estimated. SNS Insider's software-specific data point indicates the software segment is the fastest-growing within AI inference, driven by increasing adoption of inference optimization frameworks, model compression tools, and AI-as-a-service platforms. A defensible SAM for the software/tooling sub-layer—applying a 10–15% software revenue share against the $106B total market—implies a current SAM of approximately $10–16B, scaling to $25–38B by 2030. This is still a large addressable opportunity for a seed-stage company. The SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market) for TheStage AI in the near term is best scoped as AI/ML teams at cloud-native startups and mid-market enterprises actively deploying PyTorch models on NVIDIA GPU infrastructure—a population of tens of thousands of organizations globally. The company's pricing thesis around GPU cost savings (up to 5x reduction) creates a value-linked capture opportunity sized against the $37.5B in AI cloud inference infrastructure spend that inference workloads claimed in early 2026. A 1% capture of that spending-reduction value would represent a $375M revenue opportunity, validating the market scale for a category-defining player. The analyst consensus is unambiguous: inference is the dominant and fastest-growing cost center in enterprise AI, and the software optimization layer is the highest-growth sub-segment. TheStage AI's TAM framing is directionally accurate and conservatively grounded in verifiable market intelligence.","isPainReal":"The pain of unoptimized AI inference is independently validated across multiple dimensions of market evidence. First, structural cost burden: industry reports confirm inference accounts for 80–90% of total compute costs over a production AI model's lifecycle, with the Flexera 2025 State of the Cloud Report finding that managing cloud spend remains the top challenge for 84% of organizations—with AI compute the primary driver of overruns. Over a third of businesses regularly experience cloud budget overruns of up to 40% (Pepperdata, cited in TechCrunch coverage of Deci). Second, M&A validation: NVIDIA has serially acquired inference optimization startups—Deci AI (est. $300M, May 2024), OctoAI/OctoML (acquired 2024–2025), and CentML ($400M+, June 2025)—specifically to internalize GPU optimization software that deepens its hardware moat. Red Hat (IBM) acquired Neural Magic to optimize models on commodity processors and GPUs. These are not defensive or exploratory acquisitions; they are strategic integrations of optimization software that NVIDIA recognizes as existentially important to its GPU value proposition. Third, venture consensus: Inferact raised $150M seed at $800M valuation from a16z and Lightspeed to commercialize vLLM inference optimization, explicitly targeting the 'inference gap' where companies struggle to run models efficiently in production. Modular raised $380M total ($250M Series C at $1.6B valuation) to build a unified AI inference compute layer. CentML raised $27M seed from Gradient Ventures, NVIDIA, and Microsoft for ML cost optimization before its $400M+ NVIDIA exit. Fourth, developer pain benchmarks: Adobe achieved a 60% reduction in diffusion latency using NVIDIA Model Optimizer with TensorRT-LLM—demonstrating the magnitude of efficiency gains available through software optimization alone. TheStage AI's own published case study with Recraft.ai demonstrated 2x model performance improvement and 20% latency reduction via quantization and sparsification on NVIDIA H100 GPUs, with a significant reduction in the number of GPUs needed for inference—a directly observable cost saving. Fifth, the optimization engineering talent gap: manual model compression at the level required for production deployment requires rare expertise in discrete mathematics, hardware compiler engineering, and quantization theory. TheStage AI's team—four PhD-level founders who spent a decade at Huawei optimizing on-device models for flagship smartphones (P50, P60) and published an award candidate paper at CVPR 2023 on Integral Neural Networks—represents exactly the caliber of expertise that cannot be internally replicated at most AI startups. The combination of recurring cost pain, M&A validation, elite VC consensus, and demonstrated technical outcomes from TheStage AI's own customer base constitutes a high-confidence validation of the pain's reality, depth, and commercial urgency.","tractionFunnel":[{"stage":"Negotiation","logo":"Recraft.ai","details":"Confirmed paying customer.
