Generation of Inference Infrastructure \nThrough Hybrid and On-device Inference\n--- Page 2 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nTABLE OF CONTENTS \nTable of contents  1\nIntroduction  2\nProduct  3\nBusiness Model  5\nMarket  7\nGTM  7\nCompetition  7\nUnfair advantages  8\nVision  9\nRisks / FAQ 11\n1\n\n--- Page 3 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nINTRODUCTION \nFacts on DNNs inference DNNs (Deep Neural Networks) are widely applied to real \nworld problems like: Image/Video enhancement, NLP, segmentation, detection, \nautonomous driving planing and so on. Enterprise production DNNs always goes \nthrough compression & acceleration step. Compression & acceleration step is hard \nand always done manually through a lot of brute-force by AI engineers to preserve \nquality while achieving desired model size or inference time for specific hardware. \nWhat we do? TheStage AI is building an AI platform for engineers and researchers that \nsignificantly speeds up their R&D in DNN acceleration and deployment. The platform \nsupports a wide range of inference platforms, including integration with customers' \nexisting infrastructure on clouds like AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud. From a business \nperspective, it reduces inference infrastructure costs and enables AI teams to achieve \ntheir KPIs more efficiently. \nWhich problems we are solving? We are reducing GPU costs, accelerating response \ntimes of DNNs, and making DNNs smaller in a fully controllable manner on customer \ninfrastructure with a unique and easy-to-use UX on our platform app.thestage.ai. Our \nproduct efficiently answers the following questions: \n \"What's the maximum AI model acceleration and cost reduction I can achieve with \ngiven AI model quality restrictions?\" \n \"What's the maximum quality I can get from my AI model with given GPU cost \nrestrictions?\" \n \"How can I deploy an accelerated AI solution to my infrastructure without \nmigrations or headaches?\" \n \"What's the best GPU/Device for my AI model, workload and how to run \naccelerated inference on those devices?\" \nHow we are solving these problems? We've developed a mathematical framework to \nefficiently accelerate AI models, balancing quality, size, and inference time. Our \nPython framework and TheStage AI platform oﬀers an intuitive workspace for AI \nengineers and researchers. Our technology, akin to ZIP and JPEG compression, \n2\n--- Page 4 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nidentifies redundancies in model parameters and input data. It allows users to specify \ndesired model size or inference time while optimising quality, all powered by \nadvanced mathematical algorithms. \nPRODUCT \nTheStage AI platform is a user-friendly workspace for AI teams to collaborate on \nmodel optimisation and deployment. Rent cloud instances, simulate inference, and \nadapt models to your data. Our intuitive interface fills a market gap, providing an \naccessible entry point for engineers to streamline AI development and preparation for \nproduction. \nUser flow  \n• The user selects a project template from specific sections based on the target \ndeployment device, such as a server GPU or an on-device processor. \n• The user then selects an execution environment, such as a container with a GPU or \nthe option to attach their GPU directly to the platform. \n• The user runs compression and compilation within the containers. \n3\n\n--- Page 5 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\n• Finally, the model is exported for use with the inference SDK. \nPlatform consists from several parts: \n• Cloud Management and renting of GPU resources for inference optimisation \nprojects. Connect your own GPUs in a secure way. Split resources with Docker GUI \nbetween team members. \n• Projects AI engineer's ideal Github. Streamlined workflow with automatic version \ncontrol and experiment tracking. Create project, connect container, and run fine-\ntuning, validation, or inference simulations instantly from your local machine, as if \ncloud GPUs were directly connected. Real-time output streaming to your terminal. \nAll runs automatically committed and easily discoverable on the platform. \n• QLIP Toolkit Intelligence core of our platform. Advanced PyTorch-based framework \nfor model compression and acceleration. Features ANNA (Automated NNs \nAccelerator) for precise model optimisation based on inference time, size, and \nquality constraints. Includes hardware-specific compiler supporting QLIP protocol. \n4\n\n--- Page 6 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nElastic models by TheStage This is a predefined set of open-source models optimised \nby ANNA. All presented models have multiple states corresponding to diﬀerent \noptimisation levels, oﬀering various trade-oﬀs between cost and quality. For instance, \nwe can quickly produce models ranging from 30B to 1B parameters from Llama 34B, \neach with optimal quality for its size. Users can start serving these models within \nseconds on their existing infrastructure across popular cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, \nNebius, etc.) using our optimised HTTP server, which integrates easily with these cloud \nservices. \nBUSINESS MODEL \nOn-device Inference SDK Priced per active device per-month.  \nCloud Inference SDK Priced per GPU hour. \nDeployment management Priced as a platform subscription. \nCUSTOMERS \nCompany Product Deployed Pricing Annual Recurring Revenue\nNebius ElasticModels  /\nTranscription \nContainers\n$75 000 \nintegration \ncontract.  \n \n800$ per GPU \nper year running \nour containers.\n$350 000 (estimated from the full \ndeployment, means that it will grow \nduring year until full deployment)\nNeuphonic ElasticModels / \nAppleSDK \n(integration of the \nNeutts model)\n12 000$ \nintegration \ncontract. \n \n+ per active \ndevice pricing\n250 000$ (estimated from the current \non-going contracts)\nBrilliant Labs ElasticModels / \nAppleSDK\n25 000$ \nintegration \ncontract \n+ per-device \npricing\n250 000$ (estimated for number of \ndevice released during 2026), flat fee \nafter 1 000 000$ of deployment.\nCompany\n5\n--- Page 7 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nREVENUE \nCumulative Revenue $190 000, main source is from integration contracts right now.  \nAnnual Recurring Revenue $900 000, estimation based on customers demand. \nRecurring Revenue Source Inference engine license fees.  \nMILESTONES \nTarget 2026 Deploy hybrid inference solution to apps with a total usage over 5 \nmillions devices per month. It will move us to more than 1 000 000$ MRR. \nTarget 2027 Deploy hybrid inference solution to apps with a total usage over 20 \nmillions devices per month. It will move us to more than 4 000 000$ MRR. \nKey costs Team salaries, GPUs. See budget deployment document. \nAlgorithm to achieve milestones Praktika already has two million monthly users \nmonthly and Brilliant Labs is launching a million devices. Our existing customer base \nand pipeline are enabling us to reach these revenue milestones. We intend to refine \nand expand this pipeline further. \nMirelo AI NvidiaInferenceEngin\ne / Compiler\n35 000$ \n+ 30 000$ \n(additional \nlicense scale, \naudit at the end \nof 2026)\n35 000$\nPraktika ElasticModels  /\nTranscription \nContainers\n$10 000 flat fee \nper year\n10 000$\nRecraft NvidiaInferenceEngin\ne / Compiler\n0$ (design \npartner)\nPayable cooperation in a discussion\nProduct Deployed Pricing Annual Recurring RevenueCompany\n6\n--- Page 8 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nMARKET \nGenAI companies constantly update their models and spend significant resources on \ninference. Our platform automates this stressful process before each production \nrelease.  \nRobotics / autonomous driving / wearables  have a lot of embedded AI models which \ncan also impact on human safety. Such a companies strongly focuses on models \noptimisations to create real-time high quality models. Our products automates this \nprocessing and gives clear understanding on model capacity! \nGaming companies Already utilises AI super-resolution technologies to improve \nquality of the rendered scene. Also LLMs and TTS will become a great part of the \ngaming which will require strong optimisations from cloud to on device inference. \nGTM \nOpen-source TheStage AI already oﬀers an open-source solution for transcription \nmodels that demonstrates world-leading performance.  Our paid features are \nimportant so this increases adoption and motivates users to tackle problems where \nour paid solution is the answer. \nIntegrations to existing ecosystems Deploying our solutions to customers like Nebius \nprovides us with a valuable distribution channel and robust support for cloud \ninference. \nLaunch partners We’ll be the launch partners for Neuphonic and Camb.AI’s closed-\nsource models.  Our aim for 2026 is to become the main launch partner for \nElevenLabs’ edge model and expand these partnerships to other great companies. \nCOMPETITION \nMiraiAI This approach focuses on creating their own inference SDK written in Metal \n(GPU). It oﬀers more control over kernels and memory allocations but blocks low-bit \ninference (weights and activations), making it slower.  Additionally, it’s not suitable for \ngames as the GPU is occupied with rendering and can’t be used for background tasks \nlike voice assistants.  Inference power consumption increases from six to ten times. \n7\n--- Page 9 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nCactus Custom kernels for the CPU are incredibly fast and allow use even when the \nGPU is occupied. However, they share the same issues as MiraiAI.  \nHardware Vendors Their core business revolves around hardware utilisation.  \nConsequently, such companies consistently strive to develop tools centred around \ninference, as this significantly enhances adoption.  They typically partner or acquire \nteams, as seen with Nexa AI’s recent acquisition by Qualcomm.  This demonstrates \ntheir satisfaction when they witness a growing community improving their ecosystem \nand promoting it.  Another observation is that Nvidia doesn’t build acceleration and \nmodels for Apple, Apple doesn’t develop these technologies for Android phones and \nAndroid phone vendors won’t create such technology for Apple.  For developers, it’s \ncrucial to have a consistent experience across diﬀerent hardware, which TheStage AI \nensures. \nTheStage AI Optimises models autonomously and delivers an additional multiplier (2x \n- 4x) to all previous optimisations! We don't use handcrafted kernels or compilers; \ninstead, we've built integrations with existing compilers from hardware manufacturers \n- leveraging the current ecosystem. While hardware manufacturers provide the ability \nto run models on their hardware, they don't focus on model quality tech enough. This \nis the gap we're efficiently closing. \nTheStage AI has found their niche through solving the most hard and painful problem! \n \nUNFAIR ADVANTAGES \nHuawei experts in models acceleration Our team consists of enterprise-level experts \nin model compression and acceleration. The CEO holds several patents used in major \nHuawei products, including AI cameras in P50 and P60 smartphones. Additionally, our \nteam developed the primary quantization framework within Huawei, which was utilized \nby over 30 Huawei teams worldwide. We aim to create a unique interface for \ndesigning and adopting acceleration algorithms, as we're building a product for \nresearchers like ourselves! \nTop level research Our focus on the intersection of mathematical modeling and AI \nallows us to approach problems from a unique angle, leading to novel solutions like \ninn.thestage.ai - our award paper from top AI conference CVPR2023. The main \n8\n--- Page 10 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nfounders team have PhDs in complicated fields like: math, neuroscience, computer \nscience. \nGeneral model compression & acceleration protocol During 5 years of our work in \nacceleration filed we have found that compression & acceleration protocol can be \ndesigned in a hardware agnostic way by properly converting quantised or sparsified \nrepresentation to a specific compiler representation. With our implementation we can \nmove fast with new hardware as well as with new algorithms. \nAutomatic NNs acceleration and Elastic models It's really important to have such \nmodes for running AI! Just compare it with YouTube videos that have several resolution \noptions: 360p, 480p, 720p, etc. The same goes for games—if you don't have enough \nGPU power, you adjust graphics rendering to low or medium mode by moving a slider. \nDo you see these analogies? AI will be the same, but nobody sees this currently! \nFurthermore, AI will oﬀer a wider range of customisable modes tailored to each user's \nneeds! \n  \nSimple integration with customer infrastructure It is important to not create \nobstacles with migration with existing AI products. We have already checked it a lot of \ntimes! \n      \nUnique approach for experiments workflow We are building workflow for \noptimisation and deployment which appreciated by research engineers as we can \nunderstand their pains as nobody! \nBy leveraging these unique advantages, TheStage AI is positioned to revolutionize AI \nefficiency and deployment, potentially transforming industries from edge computing \nto next-generation internet technologies. \nVISION \nStandard technology lifecycle Looking at technologies like the internet or \ncomputing, we can observe their development from a centralized model—initially \nused only by tech companies—to a \"cloud\" phase. This phase included computer and \ninternet clubs where people paid for access by the hour (currently its GPU clouds). As \nthese technologies became more appealing for mass adoption, centralised \nmanagement became less beneficial. Consequently, internet and computers moved \ninto our homes and smartphones, sparking a global digitalisation process. This shift \nled to the creation of efficient solutions such as new hardware, operating systems, \n9\n--- Page 11 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\ndata compression techniques, and tools. These innovations became standard for \nmany developers and engineers, who continue to optimise them, creating more \nopportunities for personalised experiences. TheStage AI, having been early adopters \nof AI technologies in enterprises like Huawei, believes they are building one of the \nworkspaces and tech that will be utilised and transformed in the near future, \npotentially creating a multi-billion dollar opportunity! \n1-2 years future We believe the current AI landscape will create numerous new job \nopportunities for AI engineers who adapt AI technologies for internal enterprise use or \nprovide end-to-end customer services. This mirrors the evolution of Python. While \nGoogle invented the Python programming language, they didn't create PyCharm or \nJupyter Notebook—tools that have become essential workspaces for developers and \nresearchers. Additionally, robotics and autonomous driving are advancing rapidly. \nThese systems often employ multiple DNNs to work in multimodal scenarios, handling \nvarious tasks that can be sensitive to human life. Such neural networks must be fast, \nenergy-efficient, and comprehensible in terms of quality variations—challenges our \nproduct addresses! \n2-5 years future  We also believe AI will have a significant social impact from various \nperspectives. Firstly, AI will become a personal companion through Retrieval-\nAugmented Generation (RAG) on private cloud storage and with Deep Neural \nNetworks (DNNs) living and updating on your devices. Humans constantly seek \nsuggestions, understanding, and deeper insights into their problems, which people \ncan't always address adequately. As AI develops, your AI assistant will become an \nincreasingly intelligent friend who understands you better. You'll also be able to create \nplugins for them to quickly learn new skills. We believe the simple, intuitive interface \nwith sliders that TheStage AI is building will allow everyone to modify their neural \nnetworks as easily as applying Instagram filters to a photo—a task that required \ncomputer and Photoshop skills just 20 years ago! \nAnother impact will be on internet communication. The internet has always involved \ndata compression, and AI can do it 10 times more efficiently, potentially moving us \nfrom 5G to 10G! However, this will require special chipsets to compress and \ndecompress data on your device, similar to how current CPUs have dedicated chips \nfor video, speech, and music decoding. These chips began development 20-30 years \nago, and a new wave of compression specialists became patent shareholders for \ncompression algorithms along with hardware. I believe TheStage AI will collaborate \nwith 3GPP and other compression consortiums to create a new AI-powered internet \nwith special chips. Over the next few years, we'll explore various DNN architectures, \n10\n--- Page 12 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nlearning how to make them optimization-friendly and determining the necessary chip \ncharacteristics. Currently, H.265 codec chips or 3GPP VoIP chips have billions of \nlicenses used by Intel, AMD, Qualcomm, ARM, Nvidia, and others, with all these \ncompanies paying to use these chipsets with provided compression algorithms! \nRISKS / FAQ \nRapid AI acceleration advancements \nThe pace of new optimization techniques is accelerating — this is not a risk, it’s the \ncore driver of our value. \nANNA is built as an aggregation and decision layer, not a single-method solution. As \nnew SOTA techniques emerge, they are integrated into our system and automatically \nevaluated through a multi-dimensional search across model layersto optimize for \nquality and latency. \n→ The faster the field evolves, the more valuable a unifying optimization layer \nbecomes. \nRapid evolution of AI hardware (NPUs, accelerators) \nHardware is improving ежегодно, but model complexity is scaling faster. This creates \na permanent gap between theoretical and real-world performance. \nIn practice: \n• Unoptimized models are never deployed at scale \n• New hardware arrives with immature software stacks \n• Fragmentation across devices increases integration cost \n•→ We are not dependent on a specific chip cycle - we benefit from all of them by \nacting as the abstraction layer that makes hardware usable. \nAdoption risk \nAdoption is a function of friction. Most infrastructure products fail because they \nrequire system rewrites — we do the opposite. \nWe integrate directly into existing pipelines with minimal code changes, augmenting \nrather than replacing current systems. \n11\n--- Page 13 ---\nINVESTMENT MEMO THESTAGE AI\nThis approach is grounded in execution: the core quantization framework built by our \nteam at Huawei won internal adoption in a competitive environment and was used in \nproduction systems. \n→ Proven ability to ship and drive adoption inside a highly demanding engineering \norganization. \nCommoditization of optimization \nOptimization in AI does not converge to a static solution. \nUnlike traditional software, AI systems are continuously evolving: \n• Architectures change \n• Hardware constraints shift \n• Quality vs latency trade-oﬀs remain dynamic \nIf optimization were commoditized, model performance would also commoditize - \nwhich is inconsistent with current industry dynamics. \n→ Optimization remains a high-skill, continuously evolving layer, not a fixed utility. \nInternalization by large companies \nLarge companies can build internal solutions - and some will. This is expected, not a \nblocker. \nHowever: \n• Internal tools are rarely generalized or externalized \n• The majority of the market cannot aﬀord to replicate deep optimization stacks \n• Even large ecosystems rely on external layers when it accelerates iteration \n•At the same time, hardware vendors (e.g., Nvidia and others) are moving toward open \ncompiler ecosystems, abstracting low-level complexity while leaving higher-level \noptimization and quality tuning unsolved. \n→ This expands, rather than reduces, the opportunity for an independent optimization \nlayer.\n12","ts":1782486127498},{"name":"Corporate Bylaws - The Stage AI Inc signed.pdf","url":"/uploads/CorporateBylaws-TheStageAIIncsigned_34f27849.pdf","text":"\n--- Page 1 ---\nCORPORATE BYLAWS\nof\n______________________________________________________________\nARTICLE 1\nCompany Formation\n1.01 FORMATION.  This Corporation is formed pursuant to the General Corporation Law of the\nState of Delaware (“the Law”) and the laws of the State of Delaware.\n1.02 CORPORATE  CERTIFICATE C O M P L I A N C E .  The  Board  of  Directors  (the  “Board”)\nacknowledges and agrees that they caused the Corporation’s Certificate of Incorporation  to be\nfiled with the Delaware Department of State and all filing fees have been paid and satisfied.\n1.03 REGISTERED OFFICE & REGISTERED AGENT.  Per Sections 131 and 132 of the Law,\nthe Board agrees that the Corporation’s registered agent for service of process is  located in the\nState of Delaware, as stated in the Certificate of Incorporation. The Corporation may change its\nregistered agent by resolution of the Board and filing a statement with the Department of State\nsetting forth the change. Pursuant to Section 132 of the Law, the Board is obligated to maintain\nand update the corporate records on file with the Corporation’s registered agent.\n1.04 OTHER OFFICES.  The Corporation may have other offices as selected by the Board per\nSection 122 of the Law.\n1.05 CORPORATE SEAL.  Pursuant to Section 122 of the Law, the Board may adopt a corporate\nseal with the form and inscription of their choosing. The adoption and use of a corporate seal is\nnot required. \n1.06 PURPOSE.  Pursuant to Section 122 of the Law, this Corporation is formed to engage in any\nlawful business purpose.\n1.07 ADOPTION OF BYLAWS.  Pursuant to Section  109 of the Law, the Board  has caused the\nadoption of these corporate bylaws on behalf of the Corporation.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 1 of 14\n--- Page 2 ---\nARTICLE 2\nBoard of Directors\n2.01 INITIAL MEETING  OF  THE  BOARD.   Per  Section  108  of  the  Law,  the  Board  has\nconducted and completed the initial organizational of the Corporation.\n2.02 POWERS AND NUMBERS.  Pursuant to Sections 141 and 271  of the Law, the management\nof  all  the  Corporation’s  affairs,  property,  and  interests  shall  be  managed  by  or  under  the\ndirection of the Board.  Per Section  141 of  the Law , the Board of the Corporation shall be\ncomprised of the number of directors listed in the Certificate of Incorporation, unless expressly\naltered by these Bylaws. Consistent with Section 141 of the Law, the Board consists of at least\none (1) natural person who need not be a shareholder or resident of the State of Delaware.\n2.03 DIRECTOR LIABILITY.  Each director is required, individually and collectively, to act in\ngood faith, with reasonable and prudent care, and in the best interest of the Corporation. If a\ndirector acts in accordance with Section 141 of  the Law , then they shall be immune from\nliability arising from official acts on behalf of the Corporation. Directors are presumed to act in\ncompliance with Sections 141 of the Law.\nDirectors who fail to comply with Sections  141, 171, or 172  of  the Law  shall be personally\nliable to the Corporation for any improper distributions and as otherwise described in Sections\n172 and 174 of the Law and these Bylaws. \n2.04 CLASSES OF DIRECTORS.  Until such time as the Certificate is accordingly amended, the\nCorporation does not have classes of directors.\n2.05 CHANGE  OF  NUMBER.   The  number  of  directors  may  be  changed  at  any  time  by\namendment of these Bylaws, pursuant to the process outlined in Article 10 of these Bylaws. A\ndecrease in number does not have the effect of shortening the term of any incumbent director. In\nthe  event  the  established  number  of  directors  is  decreased,  the  directors  shall  hold  their\npositions until the next shareholder meeting occurs and new directors are elected and qualified.\n2.06 ELECTION & REMOV AL OF DIRECTORS.  Pursuant to Section 211 of the Law, directors\nare to be voted on and elected at each annual shareholder meeting for a term of one (1) year. A\ndirector shall hold office until their successor is duly elected and qualified at the following\nannual shareholder meeting , unless a special meeting is expressly called to remove a director\nand/or fill a vacancy. I f a director is elected, but is not yet qualified to hold office, then the\nprevious director shall holdover until such time that the newly elected director is so qualified.\nPursuant to Section 141 of the Law, one or more directors of the Board, may be removed by an\naffirmative  vote  by  the  holders  of  a  majority  of  stock entitled  to  vote  at  any meeting  of\nshareholders called expressly for that purpose.  \n2.07 VA C A N C I E S .   Per Section  223 of the Law, all vacancies in the Board may be filled by the\naffirmative vote of a majority of the remaining directors, provided that any such director who\nfills a vacancy is qualified to be a director and shall only hold the office until a new director is\nelected by the  shareholders at the next meeting of the  shareholders.  Any director who fills a\nvacancy on the Board shall not be considered unqualified or disqualified solely by virtue of\nCorporate Bylaws Page 2 of 14\n--- Page 3 ---\nbeing an interim director. Pursuant to Sections 141 and 223 of the Law, any director elected by\nthe shareholders to fill a vacancy which results from the removal of a director shall serve the\nremainder of the annual term of the removed director and until a successor is elected by the\nshareholders and qualified. The Board may fill a vacancy created by an increase in the number\nof directors for a term lasting until the next annual election of directors by the shareholders at\nthe annual meeting or a special meeting called for the purpose of electing directors.\n2.08 REGULAR MEETINGS.  Pursuant to Section 141 of the Law, the meetings of the Board or\nany committee may be held at the Corporation’s principal office or at any other place designated\nby the Board or its committee, including by means of remote communication which allows all\npersons participating in the meeting to hear each other at the same time. The annual meeting of\nthe Board will be held without notice immediately after the adjournment of the annual meeting\nof shareholders. \n2.09 SPECIAL MEETINGS.  Pursuant to Section 141 of the Law, special meetings of the Board\nmay be held at any place and at any time, including by means of remote communication which\nallows all persons participating in the meeting to hear each other at the same time, and may be\ncalled by the Chairman of the Board, the President, Vice President, Secretary, or Treasurer, or at\nleast two (2) directors. Any special meeting of the Board must be preceded by at least forty-\neight (48) hours' notice of the date, time, place, and purpose of the meeting, unless these\nBylaws require otherwise. \n2.10 ACTION BY DIRECTORS WITHOUT A MEETING.  Pursuant to Section 141 of the Law,\nany action which may be taken at a meeting of the Board, or its committee, may be taken\nwithout a meeting, provided all directors or committee members unanimously agree and sign a\nconsent that sets forth the action taken taken by the Board . The signed consent is to be  filed\nwith the minutes of the proceeding.\n2.11 NOTICE OF MEETINGS.  Pursuant to Section 141 of the Law, the  regular meetings of the\nBoard shall be held without notice of the date, time, place, or purpose of the meeting, provided\nthe meeting of the Board follows the adjournment of the annual shareholder meeting. Notice\nmay be given personally, by facsimile, by mail, or in any other lawful manner, so long as the\nmethod for notice comports with Article 8 of these Bylaws. Oral notification is sufficient only if\na written record of the notice is included in the Corporation's minute book. Notice is effective at\nthe earliest of: \n(a) Receipt; \n(b) Delivery to the proper address or telephone number of the director(s) as shown in the\nCorporation's records; or \n(c) Five (5) days after its deposit in the United States mail, as evidenced by the postmark, if\ncorrectly addressed and mailed with first-class postage prepaid. \n2.12 WA IV ER  OF  N OTI C E.   Pursuant to Section  141 of  the Law , a director waives the notice\nrequirement if that director attends or participates in the meeting, unless a director attends for\nthe  express  purpose  of  promptly  objecting  to  the  transaction  of  any  business  because  the\nCorporate Bylaws Page 3 of 14\n--- Page 4 ---\nmeeting was not lawfully called or convened. A director may waive notice by a signed writing,\ndelivered to the Corporation for inclusion in the minutes before or after the meeting.\n2.13 QUORUM.  Per Section 141 of the Law, a majority of the entire Board constitutes a quorum,\nand a quorum is necessary at all meetings to constitute a quorum to transact business.\n2.14 REGISTERING DISSENT.  Pursuant to Section 141, a director who is present at a meeting at\nwhich an action on a corporate matter is taken is presumed to have assented to such action,\nunless the director expressly dissents to the action. A valid dissent must be entered in the\nmeeting’s  minutes,  filed  with  the  meeting’s  acting  Secretary  before  its  adjournment,  or\nforwarded by registered mail to the Corporation’s Secretary within twenty-four (24) hours after\nthe meeting’s adjournment. These options for dissent do not apply to a director who voted in\nfavor of the action or failed to express such dissent at the meeting. \n2.15 EXECUTIVE AND OTHER COMMITTEES.  As permitted by Section 141 of the Law, the\nBoard may create committees to delegate certain powers to act on behalf of the Board, provided\nthe Board passes a resolution indicating such creation or delegation. Notwithstanding the power\nto  create  committees,  no committee  may  issue  stock,  recommend  shareholder actions,  nor\namend these Bylaws. The Board may delegate to a committee the power to appoint directors to\nfill vacancies on the Board. All committees must record regular minutes of their meetings and\nkeep the minute book at the corporation’s office. The creation or appointment of a committee\ndoes not relieve the Board or its members from their standard of care described in Section 2.03\nof these Bylaws or in Sections 141 and 172 of the Law.\n2.16 COMPENSATION.   In accordance with  Section 141 of  the Law , the Board may adopt a\nresolution which results in directors being paid a reasonable compensation for their services\nrendered as directors of the Corporation. Directors may also be paid a fixed sum and expenses,\nif any, for attendance at each regular or special meeting of such Board. Nothing contained in\nthese Bylaws precludes a director from receiving compensation for serving the Corporation in\nany other capacity, including any services rendered as an officer or employee. If the Board\naccordingly passes a resolution, then committee members may be allowed like compensation\nfor attending committee meetings. \nA resolution of the Board that grants compensation to a director may be challenged by a\nshareholder,  provided  the  shareholder requests  a  special  shareholder meeting  specifically\naddressing the resolution related to director compensation. Any Board resolution that relates to\ndirector compensation can be overturned by a majority vote of shareholders.\n2.17 LOANS.   The Corporation may not make loans to the directors without the approval of a\nmajority of the shareholders entitled to vote.  \n2.18 INDEMNIFICATION.  Provided the director complies with the standard of care described in\nSection 2.03 of these Bylaws and Section 145 of the Law, the Corporation shall indemnify any\ndirector made a party to a proceeding, brought or threatened, as a consequence of the director\nacting in their official capacity. I f a director is entitled to indemnification by the Corporation,\nthe director shall be indemnified pursuant to the process outlined in Section 145 of the Law.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 4 of 14\n--- Page 5 ---\nARTICLE 3\nStock\n3.01 AUTHORITY TO ISSUE.  Subject to Sections 151 and 161 of the Law and the Corporation’s\nCertificate of  Incorporation,  the  Corporation  is  authorized  to  issue  any  class  of  stock  or\nsecurities convertible into stock of any class. Before any stock of the Corporation may be\nissued, the Board must pass a resolution which authorizes the issuance, sets the minimum\nconsideration for the stock or security (or a formula to determine the minimum consideration),\nand fairly describes any non-monetary consideration. \n3.02 RESTRICTIONS.  Stock may only be issued in accordance with the Corporation’s Certificate\nof Incorporation, and through the process described in these Bylaws. Any issuance of stock in\nexcess  of  the  amount  described  in  the  Corporation’s  Certificate of  Incorporation  must  be\nauthorized by the Board and approved by the affirmative vote by a majority of shareholders. Per\nSections 159 and 202,  of the Law, any restriction on the transferability of stock shall be fully\nfurnished  to  the  shareholder,  upon  shareholder request,  and  without  any  charge  to  the\nshareholder. Per Sections 151 and 202 of the Law, any failure to furnish such information to the\nshareholder or a transferee without actual knowledge of the restriction renders the restriction on\nstock transferability invalid or unenforceable.\nAs provided in Sections  102 and 151 of the Law , no  shareholder has a preemptive right to\nsubscribe to any subsequent or additional issuance of stock.\n3.03 STOCK CERTIFICATES.  Under Section 158 of the Law, shareholders are entitled to stock\ncertificates  that  certify  the  shares  of  the  Corporation’s  stock  held  by  the  shareholder.\nNotwithstanding the  shareholders’ rights to stock certificates, the Board may authorize the\nissuance of some or all shares of any class or series of stock without certificates, provided the\nBoard shall provide to a shareholder a written statement that contains the information required\nto be on stock certificates, per Section 158 of the Law. \nAs required by Section 158, each stock certificate must contain on its face:\n(a) The name of the Corporation and that the Corporation is organized under the laws of\nthis State;\n(b) The name of the shareholder (or person to whom the stock is issued);\n(c) The number and class of shares and the designation of the series, if any, the certificate\nrepresents; and\n(d) The signature of two officers designated in these Bylaws or by the Board.\nFor  the  sake  of  clarity,  in  the  event  that  an  individual  serves multiple  roles  within  the\nCorporation, that person cannot countersign any document which that person has already signed\nin their official or individual capacity. If an officer who has signed or whose facsimile signature\nappears on any stock certificate ceases to be an officer before the certificate is  issued to the\nshareholder, it may be issued by the Corporation and is valid as if the person were an officer on\nthe date of issuance.  The certificate may be sealed with the Corporation’s seal.\n3.04 MUTILATED, LOST, OR DESTROYED CERTIFICATES.  Pursuant to Section 167 of the\nLaw, in the instance of any mutilation, loss, or destruction of any stock certificate, another may\nCorporate Bylaws Page 5 of 14\n--- Page 6 ---\nbe issued in its place on proof of such mutilation, loss or destruction. The Board may impose\nconditions on such issuance and may require the giving of a satisfactory bond or indemnity to\nthe Corporation. The Board may establish other procedures as they deem necessary.\n3.05 FRACTIONAL SHARES OR SCRIP.  Per Section 155 of the Law, the Corporation may: \n(e) Issue fractions of a share which entitle the holder to exercise voting rights, to receive\ndividends,  and  to  participate  in  any  of  the  Corporation’s  assets  in  the  event  of\nliquidation; \n(f) Arrange for the disposition of fractional interests by those entitled thereto; \n(g) Pay the fair market value, in cash, of fractions of a share as of the time when those\nentitled to receive such shares are determined; or \n(h) Issue scrip in a form which entitles the holder to receive a certificate for the full share\nupon surrender of such scrip aggregating a full share.\n3.06 TRANSFER.  So long as there is no transferability restriction on the stock, as described in\nSection 3.02 of these Bylaws, the stock of the Corporation is freely transferable. Transfers of\nstock must be made upon the corporation’s stock transfer books. Stock transfer books shall be\nkept in the manner described in Article 7 of these Bylaws.  \nBefore a new certificate is issued, the old certificate must be surrendered for cancellation. The\nBoard may, by resolution, open a share register in any state of the United States, and may\nemploy an agent or agents to keep such register, and to record transfers or shares therein.\n3.07 REGISTERED OWNER.  The Corporation shall recognize an individual as the registered\nowner of a given stock, provided that individual is determined as the shareholder of record by\nthe record date as set out in Section 4.07 of these Bylaws . Shareholders may agree to confer the\nright to vote or represent their stock to third parties, including trustees, proxies, or fiduciaries.\nThe Board may resolve to adopt a procedure by which a shareholder of the Corporation may\ncertify  in  writing  to  the  Corporation  that  all  or  a  portion  of  the  stock registered  in  the\nshareholder’s name are held for the account of a specified person or persons. The resolution\nmust set forth: \n(i) The classification of shareholder who may certify; \n(j) The purpose or purposes for which the certification may be made; \n(k) The form of certification and information to be contained therein; \n(l) If the certification is with respect to a record date or closing of the stock transfer books,\nthe date within which the certification must be received by the Corporation; and \n(m) Other provisions with respect to the procedure as are deemed necessary or desirable.\nUpon receipt of a certification complying with this procedure, the Corporation must treat the\npersons specified in the certification as the holders of record for the number of shares specified\nin place of the shareholder making the certification.\n3.08 CLASSES  OR  SERIES  OF STOCK.   Until  such  time  that  the  Certificate  is  amended\naccordingly, the stock of the Corporation is not classified, and is not in series. In the event the\nBoard decides to classify or reclassify the stock or alter any shareholder rights or restrictions,\nCorporate Bylaws Page 6 of 14\n--- Page 7 ---\nthen the Board shall cause an amendment to its Certificate of Incorporation to be filed with the\nCommission.  The  amendment  must  describe  the  rights  and  restrictions  which  are  being\nmodified  or altered,  along  with  a  statement  (if  any) that  the  stock  has been  classified  or\nreclassified. Pursuant to Section 151 of the Law, the amendment shall be acknowledged and\nsigned by either a director or an executive officer on behalf of the Board, or an agent authorized\nto submit filings on the Corporation’s behalf.\n3.09 STOCK OWNED BY ENTITIES.  Subject to Section 212 of the Law, stock held by another\ncorporation may be voted by that other corporation’s officer, agent, or proxy chosen by its board\nof directors, or, in the absence of such determination, by the president of that other corporation.\nPer Section 217 of the Law, shares of stock in the Corporation held by a fiduciary of the named\nshareholder may be voted or represented by the fiduciary.\nSubject to Section 160 of the Law, the Corporation may vote or represent stock that it holds in\nitself, provided  the Corporation holds such  stock in a fiduciary capacity. If the Corporation\nholds stock in itself in such a fiduciary capacity, then such stock shall be counted in determining\nthe total number of outstanding shares of stock at a given time.  \nARTICLE 4\nShareholders' Meetings\n4.01 MEETING PLACE.  Per Section 211 of the Law, all shareholder meetings must be held at the\nCorporation’s principal office or other place predetermined by the Board. As permitted by\nSection 211 of the Law, shareholders may participate in the meeting by means virtual or remote\nconference, provided the participants can hear each other in real time. \n4.02 ANNUAL MEETING TIME.  The annual  shareholder meeting for the election of directors\nand the transaction of such other business properly before the meeting, must be held each year\non ________________________, at the hour of ______________. If that date is a legal holiday,\nthen the meeting must be held on the day following, at the same hour. P er Section 211 of the\nLaw, failure to hold an annual meeting at the time stated in or fixed within these Bylaws does\nnot affect the validity of any corporate action. Per Section 231 of the Law, the Corporation shall\nappoint one or more inspectors to act at the meeting and make a written report thereof.\n4.03 ANNUAL MEETING –  ORDER  OF BUSINESS.   The  order of  business  at  the  annual\nshareholder meeting is as follows: \n(a) Calling the meeting to order; \n(b) Proof of notice of meeting (or filing of waiver); \n(c) Reading of minutes of last annual meeting; \n(d) Officer reports; \n(e) Committee reports; \n(f) Election of directors; \n(g) Disclosures to shareholders; \n(h) Miscellaneous business. \nCorporate Bylaws Page 7 of 14\n--- Page 8 ---\n4.04 SPECIAL MEETINGS.  Subject to Section 211 of the Law, special shareholder meetings may\nbe called for any purpose at any time by the President, the Board, or the Secretary. A special\nshareholder meeting may be held upon request if said request is signed, dated, and delivered to\nthe Secretary by the holders of at least one-tenth of all shares entitled to vote at the meeting.\n4.05 NOTICE.  Pursuant to Section 222 of the Law, the Secretary shall cause notice to be given to\neach shareholder of record at least ten (10) days, but no more than sixty (60) days, before the\nshareholders’ meeting. Notice shall be by electronic transmission, mailing, or personal delivery,\nand shall state the time, place, and purpose of the meeting (including instructions for how to\nvirtually  attend  and  participate).  Notice  is  considered  given  to  a  shareholder when  it  is\npersonally provided to the  shareholder, left at the  shareholder’s residence or usual place of\nbusiness, mailed to the  shareholder’s address of record, or by electronic transmission to the\nshareholder’s address or number of record on file with the Corporation. A single notice can be\ndelivered to multiple shareholders sharing the same address, unless the Corporation receives a\nrequest from a shareholder that more than a single notice be delivered.\nNotice by electronic transmission shall be considered ineffective if the Corporation is unable to\ndeliver  two  (2)  consecutive  notices  and  the  individual  responsible  for  sending  notices  to\nshareholders is made aware of the delivery failures. A shareholder meeting, and any actions\ntaken by shareholders, shall not be invalidated due to an inadvertent failure to deliver notice.\nPursuant to Section 222 of the Law and Section 4.07 of these Bylaws , the notice must include\nthe record date for determining the shareholders entitled to vote at the meeting, if such date is\ndifferent than the record date for determining shareholders entitled to notice of the meeting.\n4.06 WA IV ER  OF N OTI C E.   As stated in Section 229 of the Law, a shareholder who is entitled to\nnotice may waive the notice requirement if they provide a signed written waiver of the required\nnotice, before or after the stated meeting time, or the shareholder is present at the meeting in\nperson or by proxy and fails to object to the holding of the meeting or particular matter at the\nmeeting outside the purpose described in the notice.\n4.07 RECORD DATE.  Consistent with  Sections  213 and 219  of  the Law , at least ten (10) days\nbefore each shareholder meeting, a complete record of the shareholders entitled to vote at the\nmeeting must be made and maintained in the books and records of the Corporation. This list\nmust be arranged in alphabetical order and include the address of and number of shares of stock\nheld by each shareholder. This record must be kept on file at the Corporation’s principal office\nfor a period of ten (10) days prior to the meeting. The records must also be kept open for\ninspection at shareholder meetings.\n4.08 CLOSING OF TRANSFER BOOKS AND FIXING RECORD DATE.  Subject to Section\n213 of the Law, the Board may order the stock transfer books to be closed in order to determine\nwhich  shareholders are entitled to notice of or to vote at any  shareholder meeting, or any\nadjournment thereof, or entitled to receive payment of any dividend. Instead of closing the stock\ntransfer  books,  the  Board  may  fix  in  advance  a  record  date  for  determination  of  such\nshareholders. The record date must not be more than s ixty (60) days or less than ten (10) days\nprior to the date of the meeting, adjournment, or payment.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 8 of 14\n--- Page 9 ---\n4.09 SHAREHOLDER LIABILITY.  Consistent with Section 102 of the Law, shareholders are not\nliable to the Corporation or its creditors, except that in the event the agreed upon price or\nconsideration for the stock has not been fully paid. In the event that a subscription price or\nconsideration for stock has not been fully paid, the following people are not personally liable\nfor the unpaid balance:\n(a) a transferee or assignee who acquires the stock or subscription in good faith and without\nknowledge or notice of the nonpayment;\n(b) a person who holds the stock as a fiduciary, although the estate in the hands of the\nfiduciary is liable for the nonpayment; and\n(c) a pledgee or other person who holds stock as security.\n4.10 VOTING RIGHTS.  Pursuant to Section 212 of the Law, each outstanding share of stock is\nentitled to one (1) vote on each matter submitted to a vote at a shareholder meeting, provided\nthe voted or represented shares are held in compliance with any payment plan, subscription, or\nstock purchase agreement.\n4.11 PROXIES.  As permitted by Section 212 of the Law, a shareholder may vote either in person\nor by proxy, signed in writing by the shareholder or the shareholder’s duly authorized attorney-\nin-fact. No proxy is valid after eleven (11) months from the date signed, unless the proxy states\notherwise. A proxy is revocable by a shareholder at any time, unless the proxy states that it is\nirrevocable and is coupled with an interest.\n4.12 QUORUM.  As provided in Section 216 of the Law, the presence, in person or by proxy, of\nshareholders entitled to cast a majority of all the outstanding voting stock constitutes a quorum.\nIf a quorum is present at a  shareholder meeting, then a majority of all the votes cast at the\nmeeting is sufficient to approve any matter properly brought before the meeting.\n4.13 ACTION BY SHAREHOLDERS WITHOUT A MEETING.  Per Section 228 of the Law,\nany action which may be taken at any annual or special  shareholder meeting may be taken\nwithout a meeting if all of the shareholders entitled to vote on the subject consent to the action\nin writing. Such consent has the same force and effect as a unanimous vote of the shareholders.\nARTICLE 5\nOfficers\n5.01 DESIGNATIONS.  Consistent with  Section 141 of  the Law , the Corporation  shall have  a\nPresident, a Secretary, and a Treasurer, who will be elected by the directors at their first meeting\nafter  the  annual  shareholder meeting.  The  Corporation  may  also  have  one  or  more  Vice-\nPresidents (one shall serve as Executive Vice-President) and Assistant Secretaries and Assistant\nTreasurers as the Board may designate. An elected officer will hold office for one year or until a\nsuccessor is elected and qualified. For the sake of clarity and the avoidance of doubt , the same\nperson may hold any two or more offices concurrently, except the offices of President, Vice-\nPresident, and Secretary shall be held by at least two separate individuals.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 9 of 14\n--- Page 10 ---\n5.02 THE PRESIDENT.  Pursuant to Section 142 of the Law, the President shall preside over all\nmeetings of  shareholders and directors, shall have general supervision of the Corporation’s\naffairs, and perform all other duties as are incident to the office or are properly required by a\nresolution passed by the Board.\n5.03 VICE PRESIDENT.  During the absence or disability of the President, the Executive Vice-\nPresident may exercise all functions of the President. Each Vice-President  shall have such\npowers and fulfill such duties as may be assigned by a resolution of the Board.\n5.04 SECRETARY AND ASSISTANT SECRETARIES.  Pursuant to Section 142 of the Law, the\nSecretary must: \n(a) Issue notices for all meetings and actions of  the Board or shareholders; \n(b) Accept all requests for special meetings of the Board or shareholders;\n(c) Accept all notices of proxy appointments and revocations;\n(d) Keep the minutes of all meetings; \n(e) Accept delivery of any dissent announced at any meeting of the Board or shareholders;\n(f) Acknowledge and execute any stock certificates;\n(g) Have charge of the corporate seal and books; and \n(h) Make reports and perform duties as are incident to the office, or are properly required of\nhim or her by the Board of Directors. \nThe Assistant Secretary, or Assistant Secretaries (in the order designated by the Board), will\nperform all of the duties of the Secretary during the absence or disability of the Secretary, and at\nother times may perform such duties as are directed by the President or the Board.\n5.05 THE TREASURER.  Pursuant to Section 142 of the Law, the Treasurer shall: \n(a) Have custody of all the Corporation’s monies and securities and keep regular books of\naccount; \n(b) Disburse  the  Corporation’s  funds  in  payment  of  the  just  demands  against  the\nCorporation  or  as  may  be  ordered  by  the  Board,  taking  proper  vouchers  for  such\ndisbursements; and \n(c) Provide the Board with an account of all his or her transactions as Treasurer and of the\nfinancial conditions of the office properly required of him or her by the Board. \nIf selected, the Assistant Treasurer, or Assistant Treasurers (in the order designated by the\nBoard), must perform the duties of the Treasurer in the absence or disability of the Treasurer,\nand at other times may perform such other duties as are directed by the President or the Board.\n5.06 DELEGATION.  In the absence or inability to act of any officer and of any person authorized\nto act in their place, the Board may delegate the officer’s powers or duties to any other officer,\ndirector, or other person, subject to Section 5.01 of these Bylaws. Vacancies in any office\narising from any cause may be filled by the Board, subject to Section 5.01 of these Bylaws, at\nany regular or special board meeting.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 10 of 14\n--- Page 11 ---\n5.07 OTHER OFFICERS.  Per Section 14 1 of the Law, the Board may appoint other officers and\nagents as they deem necessary or expedient. The term, powers, and duties of such officers will\nbe determined by the Board and described in the resolution authorizing the appointment. \n5.08 LOANS.  Notwithstanding the language in Section 143 or any duty to indemnify, no loans may\nbe made by the Corporation to any officer, unless first approved by a two-thirds majority vote\nof all the outstanding the voting shares entitled to vote on the matter. \n5.09 BONDS.  The Board may resolve to require any officer to give bonds to the Corporation, with\nsufficient surety or sureties, conditioned upon the faithful performance of the duties of their\noffices and compliance with other conditions as required by the Board.\n5.10 SALARIES.  Officers’ salaries will be fixed from time to time by the Board. Officers are not\nprevented from receiving a salary by reason of the fact that he or she is also a director of the\nCorporation.\n5.11 INDEMNIFICATION.  Subject to Section 145 of the Law, officers shall be indemnified by the\nCorporation, so long as the officer acted in a manner substantially similar to and consistent with\nthe standard of care described in Section 142 of the Law. Any officer indemnification shall be\nlimited to proceedings that are directly related to or have arisen out of the officer’s acts on\nbehalf of the Corporation.\nARTICLE 6\nCapital & Finance\n6.01 DIVIDENDS.  Subject to Sections 170 and 173 of the Law, dividends may be declared by the\nBoard and paid by the Corporation out of the net earnings of the unreserved and unrestricted\nearned surplus of the Corporation, or out of the unreserved and unrestricted net earnings of the\ncurrent fiscal year, or in treasury shares of the Corporation, subject to the conditions and\nlimitations imposed by the State of Delaware. The stock transfer books may be closed by the\nBoard  and  Sections  3.07  and  4.07  of  these  Bylaws.  The  Board,  without  closing  the\nCorporation’s books, may declare dividends payable only to holders of record at the close of\nbusiness on any business day not more than sixty (60) days prior to the date on which the\ndividend is paid.\n6.02 RESERVES.  Pursuant to Section 171 of the Law, the Board may, in their absolute discretion,\nset aside out of the Corporation’s earned net surplus as they deem expedient for dividend, while\nmaintaining any corporate property, or any other purpose, before making any distribution of\nearned surplus.\n6.03 DEPOSITORIES.  The Corporation’s monies must be deposited in the Corporation’s name in a\nbank or trust company or trust companies designated by resolution of the Board. Corporate\nmonies may be drawn out only by check or other order for payment signed by such persons and\nin such manner as may be determined by resolution of the Board.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 11 of 14\n--- Page 12 ---\nARTICLE 7\nBooks and Records\n7.01 MEETING  MINUTES.   As  required  by  these  Bylaws  and  Section  224 of  the  Law,  the\nCorporation must keep a complete and accurate accounting and minutes of the proceedings of\nits shareholders and Board.\n7.02 SHAREHOLDER LIST.  Consistent with Sections 219 and 220 of the Law, the Corporation\nmust keep a list of its shareholders at its registered office, principal place of business, or other\ndesignated location. Such list must include the names and addresses of all shareholders and the\nnumber and class of shares held. \n7.03 LEGIBILITY OF RECORDS.   Any  books,  records,  and  minutes  may  be  in  any  form,\nprovided such form is capable of being converted into written form within a reasonable time.\n7.04 RIGHT TO INSPECT.   Subject to  Section 220  of  the Law , any director,  shareholder, or\nshareholder representative has the right, upon written request delivered to the Corporation, to\ninspect and copy during usual business hours the following documents of the Corporation:\n(a) The Corporate Certificate of Incorporation (initial, restated, and as amended);\n(b) These Bylaws, and any amendments; \n(c) Minutes of any proceedings;\n(d) Annual statements of affairs; \n(e) the books of account and stock ledger of the Corporation;\n(f) Any voting trust agreements;\n(g) All written communications to shareholders from the last three (3) years;\n(h) Accounting records of the Corporation; and\n(i) Record of the shareholders.\nThe Corporation elects to assume any obligations that may be related to this Article of these\nbylaws which  would  otherwise  attach  to  the  registered  agent  of  the  Corporation.  The\nCorporation  acknowledges  and  agrees  that  any  obligation  to  produce  corporate  documents\nunder this Article of the Bylaws shall attach to the Secretary as part of the duties described in\nSection 5.04 of these Bylaws.\nARTICLE 8\nNotices\n8.01 MAILING OF NOTICE.  Except as may otherwise be required by law, any notice to any\nshareholder or director may be delivered personally or by mail. If mailed, the notice will be\ndeemed to have been delivered on the close of business of the third business day following the\nday  when  deposited  in  the  United  States  mail  with  postage  prepaid  and  addressed  to  the\nrecipient’s last known address in the records of the Corporation.\nCorporate Bylaws Page 12 of 14\n--- Page 13 ---\n8.02 E-NOTICE PERMITTED.  Any communications required by the Law, these Bylaws, or other\nlaws may be made by digital or electronic transmission to the recipient’s known electronic\naddress or number as known to the Corporation at the time of notice. \n8.03 DUTY TO NOTIFY.  All shareholders, directors, officers, employees, and representatives of\nthe Corporation are required to notify the Corporation of any changes to the individual’s contact\ninformation. Pursuant to the obligations under this Section of these Bylaws, the individual must\nnotify the Corporation that electronic transmissions of notice are impracticable, impossible,\nfrustrated, or otherwise improper and ineffective.\nARTICLE 9\nSpecial Corporate Acts\n9.01 EXECUTION  OF  WRITTEN  INSTRUMENTS.   All  contracts,  deeds,  documents,  and\ninstruments that acquire, transfer, exchange, sell, or dispose of any assets of the Corporation\nmust be executed by the President to bind the Corporation. This Section does not apply to any\nchecks, money orders, notes, or other financial instruments for direct payment of corporate\nfunds which are subject to Section 9.02 of these Bylaws.\n9.02 SIGNING OF CHECKS OR NOTES.  All authorizations to distribute, pay, or immediately\ndraw upon the financial resources of the Corporation must be signed by the Treasurer, including\nany  expense  reimbursement  or  compensation  payments  to  directors,  officers,  employees,\nrepresentatives, service providers, or contractors of the Company.\n9.03 SPECIAL SIGNING POWERS.  To duly bind the Corporation to an agreement or instrument\nin the event  the  President  holds an interest  which exists outside of the  capacity  of being\nPresident, then any agreement involving such interest must be signed by an officer pursuant to\neither Section 5.03 or 9.02 of these Bylaws.\n9.04 SHAREHOLDER APPROV AL.  Pursuant to Section 102 of the Law, and until these Bylaws\nrequire otherwise, no  shareholder approval is required to acquire, transfer, exchange, sell, or\ndispose of any assets of the Corporation in the ordinary course of business or after dissolving\nthe Corporation. Notwithstanding any other provisions of these Bylaws, and consistent with\nSection  102  of  the Law , shareholder approval is required prior to any non-routine business\noperations, such as a merger, consolidation, share-exchange, conversion, or dissolution, and any\nloans that may be provided under Section 5.08 of these Bylaws.\n9.05 MERGERS & CONVERSIONS.  Following the approval from the shareholders, in order for\nany consolidation, merger, conversion, or other organizational restructuring to be effective, it\nmust follow the respective process(es) set out in Sections 251 through 267 of the Law.\n9.06 DISSOLUTION.  Following the approval of the shareholders, in order for the Corporation to\nproperly be dissolved, it must follow the process set out in  Sections 274 through 282  of  the\nLaw.\nCorporate Bylaws V i d i .\nBuild t he f ast est and most cost -eff ectiv e \nAI applications on y our infrastructur e \nwit h TheStage AI\ncontact@t hestage.ai","ts":1782486134298}],"competitiveMapping":{"summary":"TheStage AI operates in a bifurcated competitive landscape: cloud inference serving platforms (Baseten, Fireworks AI) dominate the high-growth cloud-GPU optimization layer via managed infrastructure and API abstraction, while hardware-locked on-device optimization tools (Qualcomm AI Hub, formerly Nexa AI) compete on the edge/NPU deployment vertical. The critical distinction is GTM and architectural philosophy: cloud-serving incumbents optimize throughput and serving economics at scale via vLLM/TensorRT forks and custom CUDA kernels (FireAttention), but do not perform automated model compression or cross-hardware abstraction—they assume the model as given. TheStage AI's ANNA/QLIP stack instead operates upstream, autonomously compressing and restructuring the model itself (quantization, pruning, 2:4 sparsity, int4/int8) before deployment, delivering a hardware-agnostic output that runs on Nvidia Cloud, Apple Silicon NPUs, and Jetson simultaneously—a capability no single cloud serving platform replicates. Neural Magic (Red Hat) represented the closest direct compression analog but was absorbed and deprecated in January 2025, pivoting entirely to vLLM serving, effectively vacating the automated model compression ISV niche. Qualcomm AI Hub (with acquired Nexa AI) is the strongest edge-optimization competitor, but is constitutionally Qualcomm-hardware-locked and cannot serve Nvidia cloud or Apple/cross-vendor deployments—exactly the cross-platform unified deployment layer TheStage AI targets. The market therefore splits along two axes: deployment target (cloud-GPU vs. on-device/edge) and automation depth (model compression vs. serving orchestration), and TheStage AI is uniquely positioned at the intersection of all four quadrants with a unified stack, representing a whitespace that legacy cloud serving incumbents are structurally unable to fill and hardware vendors are commercially disincentivized to build.","competitors":[{"name":"Baseten","segment":"Incumbent","valuation":"$13B (Series F, June 2026)","traction":"$600M ARR (Q1 2026); 20x YoY revenue growth; 1B+ daily inference calls; 87 global clusters","clients":"Cursor, Notion, Abridge, OpenEvidence, HeyGen, Descript, Writer, Patreon","investors":"Altimeter Capital, Conviction, Spark Capital, IVP, CapitalG, Greylock, Nvidia, BOND","gtmStrategy":"Developer-led PLG via Truss OSS; enterprise direct sales; AWS SCA partnership","differentiation":"Serving-layer only; no model compression; managed GPU orchestration, not optimization-layer","techStack":"Python, Truss framework, Firecracker micro-VMs, vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM, AWS/GCP/18 clouds"},{"name":"Fireworks AI","segment":"Incumbent","valuation":"$15B (talks, May 2026); confirmed $4B Series C Oct 2025","traction":"$800M ARR est. Manual model compression—quantization, pruning, sparsification—requires months of specialized PhD-level engineering per model per hardware target, creating a critical bottleneck as GenAI companies must re-optimize models for every new architecture, hardware generation, or latency constraint. The $106B AI inference market (2025) is growing at ~19% CAGR toward $255B by 2030, yet the software optimization layer—the highest-margin, fastest-growing subsegment—remains fragmented, manual, and inaccessible to most AI teams. The proliferation of hybrid cloud-plus-edge deployment (iOS NPUs, Jetson, Apple Silicon) compounds fragmentation: no single vendor automates cross-hardware optimization end-to-end. With AI inference spending accelerating and hardware diversity expanding, the cost and time-to-production gap is structurally widening—creating urgent, recurring demand for an automated optimization abstraction layer.","companyOverview":"TheStage AI delivers an automated inference acceleration stack targeting AI/ML teams deploying PyTorch models on Nvidia cloud GPUs, Apple Silicon, and Nvidia Jetson edge devices. Its core IP, ANNA (Automated NNs Accelerator), uses discrete math and multi-dimensional search across model layers to auto-select optimal combinations of quantization, pruning, and sparsification—compressing models in hours rather than months while preserving quality under user-defined latency or size constraints. The QLIP framework handles the full train-compress-compile-serve lifecycle; Elastic Models provide pre-optimized open-source checkpoints deployable in seconds; and a proprietary iOS/Android/macOS SDK enables on-device NPU inference for voice agents and wearables. Unlike hardware-vendor tools (TensorRT, CoreML) that are platform-locked, or inference API providers (Fireworks, Modal) that ignore model-level optimization, TheStage AI acts as a hardware-agnostic optimization abstraction layer—delivering an additive 2x–4x multiplier on top of existing compiler stacks and integrating directly into customer GPU clusters or CI/CD pipelines without data egress.","willingnessToPay":"• Direct GPU cost reduction: Validated 2.5x–8x inference cost savings (Glam AI 2.5x, Nebius production deployment, Mochi video benchmark showing 42% cost reduction vs. PyTorch baseline on H100/B200) directly translate to five- to six-figure monthly savings at scale—creating immediate, quantifiable ROI that justifies contract value.\n• Engineering labor displacement: Manual DNN compression requires months of senior AI engineer time per model per hardware target; ANNA automates this to hours, freeing scarce and expensive ML talent for product work rather than infrastructure tuning.\n• On-device cost arbitrage: TheStage SDK enables hybrid inference shifting workloads from $2.95/GPU-hour cloud to near-zero-cost NPU on consumer devices—critical for wearable, mobile, and voice AI companies (Brilliant Labs, Neuphonic, CAMB.AI) targeting millions of active devices where cloud inference economics are unsustainable.\n• Time-to-production compression: Automated optimization eliminates a 2–6 month pre-production bottleneck before each model release cycle, directly accelerating product iteration velocity for GenAI application builders.\n• Hardware fragmentation tax: As customers must deploy across Nvidia (cloud), Apple Silicon (macOS/iOS), and Jetson (robotics/edge) simultaneously, a unified cross-hardware optimization layer eliminates redundant engineering investment across each platform silo.\n• Inference quality control: ANNA's constraint-aware optimization (e.g., 'max quality at X latency' or 'minimize cost at Y accuracy floor') gives AI teams deterministic control over the speed/quality/cost tradeoff—eliminating guesswork in production SLA management.","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Three-tier hybrid model: (1) Inference Engine License — $1,000/GPU/year for cloud deployments (e.g., Nebius billed at ~$800/GPU/year in production); (2) On-device SDK — per active device per month, enabling usage-based ARR scaling with customer device fleets (Brilliant Labs targeting 1M+ devices, Neuphonic targeting 250K ARR); (3) Platform Subscription — $150/month/team base for the ANNA + QLIP acceleration platform including project management, GPU rental, and deployment tooling. Enterprise customers typically begin with an integration contract ($15K–$75K one-time) plus an annual license fee that scales with GPU count or active device volume. Blended ACV is anchored by infrastructure and application-layer customers: cloud-GPU customers (Nebius-tier) anchor at $350K+ ARR; mid-market AI startups (Glam, Praktika-tier) range $30K–$200K ARR; SMB/early-stage teams begin at $15K–$25K. Weighted pipeline-blended ACV estimated at $55,000.","estimatedAcv":55000,"unitName":"ai product teams","tamUnits":60000,"samUnits":18000,"somUnits":700},"methodology":"TAM: Derived from ~90,000 active AI companies globally (Ascendix/StartUs 2025) filtered to teams actively deploying production inference workloads on cloud or edge hardware—estimated at ~60,000 distinct AI product teams globally (excludes pure-research, pre-product, and non-inference-facing AI firms). SAM: Constrained to English-speaking markets (US, EU, Israel, UK) where TheStage AI has active GTM presence, targeting PyTorch-native teams in GenAI applications, voice AI, video generation, robotics, wearables, and AI infrastructure—verticals directly represented in their 70+ company pipeline. North America accounts for ~41% of global ML deployments; combined US/EU/Israel addressable cohort estimated at ~18,000 teams. SOM: Derived bottom-up from company milestones: internal targets of $1M MRR by end-2026 (5M devices/month) and $4M MRR by 2027 (20M devices/month) imply ~700–900 paying customer teams at blended $55K ACV over a 3–5 year horizon. Cross-checked against current signed pipeline ($190K cumulative ARR, $1.18M potential ARR in active pilots) and 6 signed deals in <18 months, projecting a ~3x annual pipeline conversion rate with expanded GTM post-$10M raise. SOM of 700 teams represents ~3.9% SAM penetration—conservative for a seed-stage B2B infrastructure company with proven production deployments and open-source distribution flywheel."}},{"geo":"EU","priority":"Medium","founders":[{"name":"Tristan Stérin","linkedin":"https://www.linkedin.com/in/tristan-st%C3%A9rin-4b37b9107/"}],"assets":[],"preemption":false,"preemptionModel":null,"id":"3ah1elp9","name":"Tristan Stérin","url":"https://www.linkedin.com/in/tristan-st%C3%A9rin-4b37b9107/","oneLiner":"Co-founder/CRO @ prgm.dev (tech startup studio, Paris); PhD CS, ENS; creator of bbchallenge","sector":"Deep Tech / Dev Tools","stage":"Pre-seed","source":"Manual","status":"Contacted","overview":{"product":"prgm.dev is a Paris-based tech startup studio and custom software design firm (founded 2021, 2–10 employees) where Tristan serves as CRO. The gap between academic proof-system expertise (Coq, Lean, Isabelle) and commercially deployable software is wide, structurally under-served, and widening as the EU AI Act and AI safety mandates drive compliance pressure across high-assurance sectors.","companyOverview":"prgm.dev is a Paris-based deep-tech startup studio and custom software firm (est. 2021) led by Tristan Stérin — ENS PhD, creator of bbchallenge, and lead of the Coq-verified BB(5)=47,176,870 proof. Stérin's unique researcher-entrepreneur profile gives prgm.dev rare credibility with AI safety labs, defense-adjacent tech firms, and computability/formal methods communities — enabling client trust that generic studios cannot replicate.","willingnessToPay":"• Regulatory compulsion: EU AI Act (2026) mandates governance documentation for high-risk AI deployments, driving immediate formal verification budgets in aerospace, fintech, and medtech.\n• Error-cost avoidance: Formal verification eliminates entire classes of software bugs; DARPA-cited case studies document high ROI for mission-critical systems where a single defect can cost millions.\n• Talent scarcity premium: Survey data shows 71.5% of formal methods experts cite lack of trained engineers as the primary adoption barrier — clients pay a significant premium for certified researcher-practitioners like Stérin.\n• Reputational signal: Association with the BB(5) Coq proof (Quanta Magazine, Scott Aaronson endorsement, ~96 Google Scholar citations) serves as a verifiable quality signal for technical buyers in AI safety and computability.\n• AI-code verification demand: As LLM-generated code proliferates, enterprises increasingly need formal proof of software correctness rather than test coverage — a structural new budget line.\n• Startup studio upside: prgm.dev's studio model creates equity co-investment optionality, increasing effective WTP beyond pure service fees.","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Project-based annual retainer with milestone delivery gates: typical engagement is a 6–18 month bespoke software or formal verification project billed as a fixed-fee annual contract, with optional recurring platform maintenance retainer. Blended ACV of $120K × 18,000 units = ~$2.16B TAM, consistent with the formal verification copilot market ($1.12B in 2024, 18.6% CAGR) plus broader custom software verification services ($7.87B globally; EU share ~25% ≈ $1.97B). SAM: Filtered to funded deep-tech startups, AI safety labs, research-adjacent enterprises, and aerospace/defense/fintech firms in France and key EU hubs (Paris, Berlin, London adjacency) actively seeking formal methods or high-assurance custom software — estimated at ~3,500 qualifying client engagements over a 3-year window, representing ~13% of EU deep-tech universe. Agentic AI has unlocked task-level automation but lacks the causal, cross-functional substrate required to execute high-stakes, irreversible decisions at enterprise scale. Three macro forces now converge: (1) geopolitical and tariff volatility is compressing decision horizons from years to months with higher stakes; (2) headless ERP/CRM architectures and MCP protocols have made real-time operational data structurally accessible for the first time; and (3) the demonstrated value of task agents (Glean, Cursor, Agentforce) has shifted board-level appetite toward the next frontier — autonomous decision execution. The gap between existing intelligence tooling and production-grade decision deployment is the defining enterprise AI white space of 2025–2028.","companyOverview":"Ergodic.ai builds 'Enterprise World Models' — a causal AI platform that ingests event traces and operational telemetry from ERP/CRM systems to construct a live, queryable business context graph.
