Chris has deep US and Europe fintech network and backed Payhook and Lane early.\nSchedule call with Chris for Thursday 26th June at 4:30 PM UK time (Simon)\nAndrawes confirmed Thursday works; Simon to send the calendar invite.\nPrepare notes on Compute Index for Chris (Simon)\nInclude competitive landscape (OARN, Silicon Data) and business model overview ahead of the intro call.","summary_markdown":"### Compute Index: Background and Team\n\n- Andrawes built the largest GPU stack at his bank, generating nine-figure P&L, before Meta approached him on compute deployment\n- Left to found Compute Index, framing compute as a financial asset class with no efficient market\n- Co-founders: David (quant background, ex-EP Research at Winton, then Citadel) and one other\n- 10 people total, team split between quants and GPU infrastructure engineers\n- Backed by Crucible Capital, Innovia Capital (Canadian firm, deal via their Canada team), and Menlo Ventures\n\n### Market for Compute Trading\n\n- Core thesis: compute is being traded by the world’s largest companies but lacks a formalized, efficient market\n- No good mechanism to move risk between market participants, the way capital markets do for other asset classes\n- More chips entering the market is net positive: more indices, more instruments, more trading volume\n\n### Business Model\n\n- Deepest compute pricing dataset, sourced by pulling data directly from invoicing systems of major neo-clouds and compute brokers\n- Built a family of regulated indices: 3 tickers live on Bloomberg, reaching 350,000 CFOs and CIOs\n- Licensed indices to 3 futures exchanges (one leaked to Bloomberg front page: Architect Financial confirmed)\n  - CFTC clearance imminent; once live, exchange and clearing fee revenue kicks in automatically\n  - Revenue on autopilot: traders don’t need to know Compute Index exists\n- Physical settlement infrastructure: acts as buyer to seller and seller to buyer to intermediate compute transactions\n  - Already processed several million dollars in compute transactions since launch a few weeks ago\n  - Just crossed $1M in revenue (index licensing + physical settlement)\n- Current bottleneck: balance sheet size caps transaction volume; actively turning away customers whose deals are too large\n\n### Fundraise\n\n- Raised $7M (closed November 2025)\n- Planning a Series A (or “big seed”), targeting \\~$30M, split roughly:\n  1. $10M: regulatory reserves (some hurdles require $10M cash/cash equivalent)\n  2. $10M: operations (compliance, risk, engineering, go-to-market)\n  3. $10M: balance sheet expansion to intermediate larger transactions\n- Timeline: targeting start in July (materials still being prepared; Andrawes just returned from his wedding)\n- Key ask from investors: connectivity to broker-dealers, financial institutions, market makers, hedge funds, and family offices\n\n### Competition\n\n- Main competitors: OARN and Silicon Data (both roughly same age as Compute Index)\n- Andrawes: “crushing them in absolutely every” dimension\n\n### Next Steps\n\n- **Prepare notes on Compute Index for Chris** (Simon)\n\n  Include competitive landscape (OARN, Silicon Data) and business model overview ahead of the intro call.\n\n---\n\nChat with meeting transcript: [https://notes.granola.ai/t/206f2649-239a-407c-a7b2-0a3072713d73](https://notes.granola.ai/t/206f2649-239a-407c-a7b2-0a3072713d73)"}],"marketStudy":{"problemThesis":"The GPU compute market is a multi-trillion-dollar asset class transacted bilaterally, with zero standardized pricing infrastructure. Hyperscalers alone are projected to spend $5.3T on AI infrastructure through 2030, yet GPU rental pricing remains opaque, bilateral, and unhedgeable — a structural gap analogous to energy markets before WTI futures existed. No standardized benchmark, forward curve, RFQ workflow, or physical settlement layer exists at scale, leaving GPU providers, lenders, and buyers unable to price risk, hedge exposure, or underwrite compute assets with institutional rigor. The resulting friction inflates cost of capital, suppresses transaction velocity, and caps the financialization of the fastest-growing infrastructure asset class in history. The emergence of CME, ICE, and Architect Financial all launching compute futures in 2025–2026 validates the macro demand signal for compute financial infrastructure.","companyOverview":"Compute Desk (operating as Compute Index) is the financial infrastructure layer for GPU compute as an asset class. Its wedge is a proprietary pricing dataset sourced directly from invoicing systems of major neo-clouds and compute brokers — yielding the deepest, most defensible private-market price intelligence on Hopper/Blackwell/AMD deals. On this data foundation, Compute Desk has built: (1) a regulated family of compute indices with 3 live Bloomberg tickers reaching ~350K CFOs/CIOs; (2) index licensing agreements with 3 futures exchanges (including Architect Financial), with CFTC-cleared exchange and clearing fee revenue imminent; and (3) a physical settlement infrastructure that acts as principal buyer/seller to intermediate compute transactions, having processed several million dollars in volume within weeks of launch and crossing $1M in revenue. The team uniquely bridges quant finance (co-founder ex-EP Research at Winton, Citadel) and GPU infrastructure engineering, enabling both cash-settled and physically-settled instruments.","willingnessToPay":"• Neo-cloud providers and GPU lessors: Hedging compute revenue volatility (H100 Blackwell spot prices surged 48% in two months per Ornn data); locked-in forward revenue curves justify premium pricing for settlement infrastructure and data feeds.\n• AI inference companies and model trainers: Large training runs costing tens of millions face catastrophic budget blowout from GPU price spikes; index-linked hedging instruments directly reduce financial risk — high ROI on instrument fees vs. unhedged exposure.\n• Lenders and infrastructure funds: $5.3T in AI capex through 2030 requires underwriting GPU-backed debt; without a credible pricing benchmark, collateral valuation is guesswork — Compute Desk's regulated indices unlock institutional lending to compute assets.\n• Exchanges (Architect, CME-equivalents): Index licensing fees are standard market structure revenue (analogous to S&P/ICE index license fees) — exchanges pay recurring royalties on every contract notional, incentivized by differentiated, CFTC-compliant data.\n• Hedge funds and family offices: As compute becomes a macro commodity, speculative and structural long/short exposure demand standardized, liquid instruments — the Bloomberg terminal presence provides instant institutional discovery.\n• GPU brokers and compute intermediaries: RFQ/bidding/escrow workflow reduces deal cycle time and counterparty risk on large transactions; platform fees are easily justified vs. deal loss from friction.","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Three interlocking revenue streams: (1) Index licensing — annual recurring fees paid by futures exchanges (Architect Financial confirmed; 2 others signed) for the right to reference Compute Desk indices in cleared derivatives contracts; revenue scales automatically with exchange notional trading volume via royalty/fee-sharing on exchange and clearing fees, requiring no incremental customer acquisition. (2) Data subscriptions — tiered institutional SaaS licenses for private-market compute pricing intelligence (Hopper/Blackwell/AMD deal data, forward curves) sold to lenders, funds, CFOs, and neo-clouds; estimated $50K–$250K ACV per institutional subscriber. (3) Physical settlement intermediation — principal spread earned on compute transactions where Compute Desk acts as buyer to seller and seller to buyer; fee estimated at 0.5%–2% of notional per transaction, scaling directly with balance sheet capacity. Blended ACV across all three streams estimated at ~$180,000 per institutional counterparty/year, anchored by the exchange licensing model and physical settlement volume.","estimatedAcv":180000,"unitName":"institutional compute market participants","tamUnits":8500,"samUnits":1800,"somUnits":220},"methodology":"TAM defined as the total universe of institutional-grade GPU compute market participants globally who transact, finance, or hedge compute at scale: ~100+ neo-clouds (McKinsey: 10–15 at scale in US, 100+ globally), ~5 hyperscalers, ~200 GPU brokers/resellers, ~300 AI inference companies with >$10M ARR, ~2,000 institutional lenders/funds with compute exposure (infrastructure PE, credit funds, family offices), ~500 enterprise CFO/treasury teams with >$5M annual compute budgets, ~300 hedge funds/proprietary trading desks entering the compute commodity space, ~6,000 smaller-scale GPU operators and colocation providers — totaling ~8,500 addressable institutional units globally. SAM (~1,800 units) scoped to the US-centric market: US-domiciled neo-clouds, AI labs, institutional lenders with GPU portfolio exposure, exchange-connected trading desks, and infrastructure funds actively deploying into AI data centers — reflecting ~36–48% US share of global GPU spend. SOM (~220 units) reflects realistic 3–5 year capture given Compute Desk's current $1M+ revenue run-rate, 3 exchange partnerships, Bloomberg terminal presence reaching 350K terminals, and physical settlement infrastructure processing several million dollars; anchored by the founder's claim of already serving multiple inference providers (Sail Research, Auto Inference, Lightly) and active pipeline of institutions too large for current balance sheet — implying near-term conversion of 200+ institutional counterparties once the Series A balance sheet expansion ($10M) is deployed."},"competitiveMapping":{"summary":"The GPU compute financialization space has crystallized around four distinct players in 2025–2026, each attacking the same greenfield opportunity — compute as a tradeable asset class — from a different structural angle. Compute Desk (as Compute Index) is the most vertically integrated: it owns proprietary invoice-level pricing data piped directly from neo-cloud billing systems, has regulated Bloomberg-listed indices, has licensed to three futures exchanges (Architect/AX, ICE, plus one undisclosed), and operates a balance-sheet-backed physical settlement desk — making it the only player that simultaneously touches data, derivatives infrastructure, and physical compute intermediation. Ornn (the closest direct threat, named explicitly by the founder) mirrors this stack but has now secured a $33M a16z-led seed round and exchange partnerships with both Architect/AX and ICE, giving it a formidable institutional distribution moat; critically, Ornn is cash-settled only and does not intermediate physical compute, ceding the settlement/balance-sheet layer to Compute Desk. Silicon Data, backed by DRW and Jump Trading and now powering CME Group's pending compute futures, is the strongest data-only incumbent with the most mature benchmark suite (SDH100RT, GPU Forward Curve, 3.5M+ daily data points, dxFeed/Refinitiv distribution), but it is explicitly positioned as a benchmarking and intelligence vendor rather than an exchange or settlement operator — it is a data supplier to, not a competitor for, the financial infrastructure role. Architect Financial Technologies ($187M valuation, $52.5M raised, former FTX US president Brett Harrison) is the exchange layer itself — it acquired a CFTC DCM, launched the American Innovation Exchange (AI Exchange), and is developing an exchange-for-physical market, but is exchange infrastructure rather than an index or settlement provider, and relies on Ornn's indices for compute pricing. The GTM split is clear: Silicon Data routes through trading-firm backers and CME institutional distribution (top-down, sell-side); Ornn routes through Bloomberg terminal reach, a16z/crypto-native LP networks, and exchange listing (sell-side + institutional DTC); Architect routes through broker-dealer and institutional onboarding (exchange-native); Compute Desk's differentiation is the private-market invoice data moat, physical settlement infrastructure, and multi-exchange index licensing with one-way exclusivity — a stack no competitor has fully replicated. The central risk for Compute Desk is execution speed: Ornn's a16z round (June 2026) and ICE partnership signal a rapidly closing capital and partnership gap.","competitors":[{"name":"Ornn","segment":"Incumbent","valuation":"Estimated $150–200M (post $33M a16z Seed, Jun 2026)","traction":"$33.7M raised total; The result is a structurally inefficient bilateral market where, as Compute Desk's own website quantifies, a single $0.01/hr pricing error on a large GPU block translates directly into hundreds of thousands of dollars of P&L leakage, and where lenders are forced to mark collateral against internal models with no secondary-market precedent. Compute Desk attacks this greenfield with a three-layer wedge — private market price intelligence sourced directly from the invoicing systems of major neoclouds and compute brokers (the hardest-to-replicate data moat), regulated index products already live on Bloomberg Terminal under three tickers reaching 350,000 CFOs and CIOs, and physical settlement infrastructure that intermediates the actual movement of compute entitlements between buyers and sellers — replicating the Bloomberg/ICE/Platts market structure playbook for an asset class that, by AUM exposed, is 2026, and Morgan Stanley forecasts cumulative investment of up to $2.9T by 2028 — all confirming a market large enough to sustain a Bloomberg/ICE-style financial infrastructure layer with index licensing, exchange fee, physical settlement, and financing revenue streams that have clear precedent at scale.","isPainReal":"• GPU compute pricing is structurally opaque and bilateral: as ICE's SVP Balance sheet size is the current binding constraint on revenue, not demand.","comparable":"Ornn: Index licensing (OCPI on Bloomberg), OTC exchange fees (Ornn Exchange), and physical capacity aggregation/margin on Ornn Compute platform; same three-pillar structure but with DeFi/prediction market integration (Kalshi, Polymarket) as an additional distribution vector."},"competitiveExposure":{"company":"Compute Desk: Direct overlap with Ornn at the","comparable":""},"incumbents":{"company":"","comparable":""}},"unitEconomicsVision":"**Unit Economics Vision**\n\nCompute Desk operates a multi-layered monetization architecture whose value metric — GPU-hour pricing intelligence derived directly from live invoicing and transaction data — sits at the intersection of three structurally distinct and compounding revenue streams: regulated index licensing, exchange and clearing fee royalties, and physical settlement intermediation spread. Each layer is priced against a different but reinforcing demand signal, and each becomes more defensible as the others scale.\n\n**Layer 1 — Index Licensing (Recurring, High-Margin, Capital-Light)**\n\nThe foundational revenue unit is the index license. Compute Desk has built what the founder describes as the deepest private-market compute pricing dataset, sourced directly from the invoicing systems of major neo-clouds and compute brokers — a methodology that captures negotiated transaction levels rather than scraped list prices. \nGPU pricing has remained largely bilateral and opaque, with posted list prices often diverging from what large buyers actually pay, and scraped indices misstating where transactions actually clear\n — making Compute Desk's invoicing-native data collection a structurally superior input versus any web-aggregation approach. This positions each index ticker as a high-confidence reference rate, analogous in function to LIBOR or SOFR for interest rates: once embedded in counterparty contracts, financing agreements, and exchange settlement mechanics, the rate becomes near-impossible to displace without systemic disruption. The pricing logic for this stream mirrors established benchmark economics: \na single benchmark can be licensed into many products, creating repeat revenue from the same underlying methodology.\n With three tickers already live on Bloomberg — reaching 350,000 CFOs and CIOs per the founder — the index is already distributed at institutional scale, and the incremental cost of servicing an additional licensed counterparty is effectively zero, driving margin asymptotically toward 100% on this stream. Critically, Compute Desk has negotiated one-way exclusivity with its exchange partners, meaning exchanges are bound to Compute Desk's indices but Compute Desk remains free to license broadly — a structurally superior position relative to competitors.\n\n**Layer 2 — Exchange & Clearing Fee Royalties (Automated, Volumetric, CFTC-Gated)**\n\nThe second and highest-potential revenue stream is royalty participation in exchange and clearing fees across three futures exchanges that have licensed Compute Desk's indices as settlement references. \nOrnn Compute Exchange raised $5.7 million in October 2025 to build what it calls the first regulated derivatives exchange for compute, publishing GPU price indices and designing cash-settled futures contracts on compute hours, structured similarly to electricity futures\n — validating the structural template Compute Desk is pursuing. The competitive dynamic is explicit: \nICE and Ornn announced plans to launch a suite of GPU compute futures contracts based on Ornn's Compute Price Index (OCPI), which tracks live-traded spot prices for GPU compute across major hardware types\n, while \nCME Group and Silicon Data are launching a futures exchange for computing capacity, with contracts based on daily GPU benchmarks for on-demand rental rates\n. Compute Desk's differentiation is its three-exchange position — versus competitors anchored to single relationships — and its physical settlement capability (see Layer 3), which provides exchange partners with a full-stack counterparty rather than a data-only vendor. The unit economics of this stream are exceptional: once CFTC clearance is obtained and futures go live, revenue accrues automatically on every contract traded or cleared, with no incremental sales motion required. The value metric for exchanges is volume-per-contract-notional, and as the founder notes, \"anyone trading on those exchanges doesn't even need to know we exist\" — a truly passive royalty structure. \nData centers need financing, lenders need valuations, AI companies need budget certainty, and investors need more direct exposure to AI infrastructure\n — each of these buyer types translates directly into futures trading volume, providing multiple demand vectors for the royalty engine. \nBlackRock CEO Larry Fink stated that a new asset class will likely be buying futures of compute given the shortage and high demand\n — a signal of the institutional capital flows that will underpin royalty volumes once contracts are live.\n\n**Layer 3 — Physical Settlement Intermediation (Balance Sheet-Constrained, Spread-Based)**\n\nThe third stream is the physical settlement business, where Compute Desk acts as principal — buyer to the seller, seller to the buyer — to intermediate real compute transactions. The pricing logic here is a bid-ask spread on GPU compute entitlements, collected per transaction, and the unit economics scale directly and mechanically with balance sheet capacity. \nAccess to credit has reportedly held back even major AI labs from investing as aggressively in new infrastructure projects as they'd like; even at the startup level, it's no trivial task to spin up tens of thousands of GPUs\n — precisely the friction Compute Desk's settlement infrastructure resolves. Within weeks of launching this infrastructure, the company had processed several million dollars in compute transactions and crossed $1M in blended revenue (licensing + settlement). The binding constraint is not demand — the founder explicitly states the company is actively turning away transactions that exceed its current balance sheet capacity — but capital: the $10M balance sheet tranche of the planned $30M raise is designed specifically to unlock larger transaction sizes and parallel deal flow. This makes the revenue model unusually legible at the unit level: every additional dollar of balance sheet deployed against a typical deal spread generates a predictable, near-term return, with revenue growing mechanically as capital scales.\n\n**Value Metric Defensibility & Compounding Logic**\n\nThe compounding dynamic across all three layers is critical to the investment thesis. \nAI infrastructure competition has entered a more sophisticated phase, the core of which is how to give highly capital-intensive assets financial measurability; data centers need financing, lenders need valuations, AI companies need budget certainty — and in this process, GPUs can become more than just hardware, serving as an asset that generates cash flow, serves as collateral, forms a price curve, and can be traded.\n Compute Desk's index is the reference rate that makes all of this possible: without a credible, transaction-anchored benchmark, there is no forward curve, no hedging contract, no financeable collateral value. \nLenders can't value contracts against market benchmarks because there are no market benchmarks, and potential competitors face enormous barriers to entry — you can't just buy a GPU futures contract to get started.\n This creates a classic benchmark network effect: as more exchanges, lenders, and compute buyers reference Compute Desk's indices in their contracts, switching costs compound and the index itself becomes systemic infrastructure rather than a product. Unlike competitors such as Ornn (now partnered with ICE and Architect Financial) and Silicon Data (partnered with CME, backed by DRW and Jump Trading), Compute Desk's differentiated moat lies in the combination of invoicing-native data sourcing, multi-exchange positioning with one-way exclusivity, and a live physical settlement layer — the latter being a capability neither Ornn nor Silicon Data appear to operate at scale, and one that creates a uniquely full-stack value proposition for exchange partners, lenders, and inference providers alike. \nA single $0.01/hr price difference amounts to hundreds of thousands of dollars in profit or loss across large Hopper, Blackwell, and AMD deals\n — illustrating that even marginal pricing accuracy commands substantial willingness-to-pay, and grounding the index's value metric in hard, quantifiable P&L impact for every counterparty it touches.","topQuestions":["Based on thorough research into Compute Desk's actual product, the competitive landscape (particularly Ornn), the regulatory environment, and the founder meeting notes, here are the sharpest diligence questions for the first partner call:","**Diligence Top Questions**","**Index methodology & data moat defensibility vs. Ornn:** You describe your pricing data as sourced directly from the invoicing systems of neo-clouds and compute brokers — what is the contractual structure and exclusivity of those data relationships?","Ornn explicitly positions its index as \"the only compute indices built solely from executed transactions, not offers, listings, or surveys,\"","and","a16z just led their $33M seed round","anchoring that exact claim. How does Compute Desk's index construction methodology differentiate from Ornn's OCPI in terms of data sourcing, coverage, and market credibility — and can your data partnerships be replicated or poached as the market matures?","**Exchange licensing strategy & revenue timing risk:** You referenced three futures exchanges licensing your indices with CFTC clearance described as \"imminent,\" and cited Architect Financial as the one that leaked publicly.","Architect acquired a Designated Contract Market and announced the \"American Innovation Exchange\" as the first CFTC-regulated exchange for futures and options on compute costs, pending regulatory review,","but notably","their announced partnership for these futures was with Ornn Data LLC, not Compute Desk.","Can you clarify which three exchanges have signed with Compute Desk, the contractual terms of those licensing agreements, and what specific regulatory milestones remain before exchange fee revenue activates — given that regulatory timelines in CFTC-adjacent markets are notoriously hard to predict?","**Physical settlement business model: counterparty risk, balance sheet mechanics, and regulatory status:** You describe acting as buyer to the seller and seller to the buyer to intermediate compute transactions — a principal intermediary model. What is the current regulatory characterization of this activity (commodity broker, swap dealer, futures commission merchant), and have you received any formal guidance from the CFTC or NFA on the required registrations?","CFTC intermediaries acting on behalf of others in connection with futures or swaps trading are generally required to register with the Commission and may be subject to various financial, disclosure, reporting, and recordkeeping requirements","— has Compute Desk received any legal opinion on whether its current physical settlement activity triggers any of these obligations prior to the next raise?","**Competitive positioning vs. Ornn's a16z-backed scale-up:** The meeting notes characterize OARN (Ornn) as a primary competitor that Compute Desk is \"crushing in absolutely every dimension,\" but the public record shows","Ornn just raised $33M in a seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz, with additional participation from a16z crypto, Galaxy Ventures, and Nordstar, closing June 24, 2026.","With Ornn now capitalized at nearly 5x Compute Desk's raise and backed by one of the most aggressive platform builders in venture, what specific product, data, or commercial advantages does Compute Desk hold today that are not replicable with Ornn's new war chest — and on what timeline does the index licensing + exchange revenue model need to scale before Ornn's distribution forecloses the market?","**Revenue quality and $1M milestone decomposition:** You referenced crossing $1M in revenue from index licensing and physical settlement since launching the settlement infrastructure \"a few weeks ago.\" What is the split between recurring index licensing revenue (which would be higher-quality, predictable SaaS-like income) versus one-time or transaction-based physical settlement fees? How many counterparties and transactions does the settlement volume represent, what are the take rates on each revenue line, and is any single customer or exchange relationship contributing a disproportionate share of that $1M — creating concentration risk?","**Regulatory capital requirements and use of the $30M raise:** You outlined a ~$10M/$10M/$10M split across regulatory reserves, operations, and balance sheet expansion.","Architect's American Innovation Exchange is being positioned as the \"definitive U.S. market\" for compute futures targeting datacenters, neoclouds, hyperscalers, and lenders","— implying significant institutional capital and compliance infrastructure behind each competing venue. What specific regulatory licenses or designations require the $10M cash reserve threshold (e.g., FCM net capital rules, swap dealer thresholds, or DCM requirements), and is the $10M balance sheet expansion sufficient to intermediate the transaction sizes you're currently turning away — or does the physical settlement business have near-term capital needs that could outpace a $30M raise?","**Team structure, founder roles, and co-founder identity discrepancy:** The meeting notes reference a 10-person team split between quants and GPU infrastructure engineers, with co-founder \"David\" described as having an EP Research background at Winton and a quant role at Citadel. However, PitchBook and public filings for Compute Desk list the co-founders as Andrawes Bahou, Nine Micheli, and Sarah Boutaud — with no \"David\" listed. Can you clarify the full founding team composition, each co-founder's equity stake and day-to-day functional ownership, whether David is an unannounced co-founder or senior hire, and how the quant and GPU infrastructure teams are structured under a company that was founded in 2025 and raised its seed only in November of that year?"],"nextSteps":["**Diligence Next Steps**","**Founder Deep-Dive Call with Chris (GP) — Compute Markets & Business Model Validation**","Schedule the intro call between Andrawes Bahou and Chris as agreed. The primary agenda should cover: (i) verification of the $1M+ revenue milestone across index licensing and physical settlement, (ii) the precise mechanics and take-rate structure across the three exchange partnerships (including the Architect Financial confirmed relationship), (iii) CFTC clearance timeline and any regulatory blockers to the futures going live, and (iv) the balance sheet intermediation model — specifically how Compute Desk underwrites counterparty risk when acting as buyer to seller and seller to buyer in physical compute settlement.","Architect has acquired a U.S. Designated Contract Market and is preparing to launch a CFTC-regulated exchange for futures and options trading on compute costs",", which makes clarifying the exclusivity and fee-share terms of Compute Desk's exchange relationship a critical due diligence item before that partnership is diluted by competing index providers.","**Competitive Positioning Teardown — Ornn vs. Silicon Data vs. Compute Desk**","Conduct a structured competitive mapping session with Andrawes to stress-test his claim of \"crushing them in absolutely every dimension.\" The key vectors to probe: (i) data sourcing methodology —","Ornn's OCPI is built from cleared trades rather than scraped list prices, spanning hardware types, regions, and contract durations",", and","Silicon Data is backed by DRW and Jump Trading Group and was designed from inception to produce compliance-grade, independently verified daily benchmarks suitable for derivatives settlement","— Compute Desk must articulate how its invoicing-system data pull is differentiated and defensible; (ii) exchange relationships —","in May 2026, ICE announced a partnership with Ornn to launch cash-settled GPU compute futures contracts referenced against the OCPI",", and","CME Group and Silicon Data announced they will launch a first-in-class compute futures market, with Silicon Data backed by global trading firm DRW","— confirm whether Compute Desk's three exchange relationships are with venues of comparable institutional liquidity depth; (iii) regulatory standing — confirm whether Compute Desk has formal IOSCO/EU BMR index certification in progress, as","Architect's AI Exchange futures contracts will be based on IOSCO- and EU BMR-certified indexes",", which is becoming a baseline expectation for institutional adoption.","**Customer Reference Calls — Inference Provider Validation**","Request introductions to at least two of the named physical settlement customers (Sail Research, Auto Inference, Lightly) for reference calls focused on: quality and utility of Compute Desk's pricing intelligence relative to alternatives, stickiness of the escrow/RFQ workflow, compute deal volume and frequency transacted through the platform, and whether they have experienced pricing/execution advantages from using Compute Desk vs. bilateral negotiation. Note that","Sail Research has just launched from stealth with $80M in funding at a $450M valuation, with Kleiner Perkins leading the Series A and Sequoia leading the seed","— this makes them a credible and fast-scaling reference customer worth verifying given both the recent funding event and their stated status as an active Compute Desk customer.","**Index Methodology & Data Integrity Review**","Request the full technical documentation on Compute Desk's index construction — specifically: how invoicing data is sourced and consented to from neo-clouds and compute brokers, how it is cleaned and normalized across heterogeneous contract structures (spot, reserved, bare-metal), refresh cadence, and methodology governance. Benchmark this against the standards set by competitors:","Ornn's OCPI is built from printed transactions rather than surveys or quotes",", and","Silicon Data's differentiation is benchmark-grade data infrastructure: daily publication, methodology transparency, independent verification, and distribution through financial terminals.","Separately, verify the Bloomberg Terminal ticker live status independently through a terminal check on the three stated tickers.","**Legal & Regulatory Diligence — CFTC, NFA, and Physical Settlement Licensing**","Engage outside counsel with CFTC/commodity derivatives experience to assess: (i) what specific regulated activities Compute Desk is currently conducting under existing licenses vs. what requires additional CFTC registration (e.g., Commodity Pool Operator, Introducing Broker, or Swap Dealer status); (ii) the $10M regulatory reserve requirement — confirm which specific regulatory hurdle triggers this and whether it is a hard pre-condition to scaling physical settlement volume; (iii) counterparty credit risk framework — since Compute Desk acts as principal (buyer to seller/seller to buyer), request their current risk limits, margin or collateral policies, and any credit losses to date across the \"few million dollars\" of transactions processed.","Ornn co-founder Kush Bavaria has stated \"from day one, Ornn has been built for regulated markets, and Kalshi is the only CFTC-regulated prediction market exchange in the US,\"","underscoring that regulatory credibility is a key differentiator in this space and one Compute Desk must demonstrate structurally rather than aspirationally.","**Cap Table & Investor Verification**","Cross-reference the investor list against what Andrawes stated in the meeting (Crucible Capital, Inovia Capital, Menlo Ventures) versus what is recorded on PitchBook (Crucible Capital, Blue Wire Capital, Drysdale Ventures, Epsilon Ventures, Sarah Smith Fund). There is a material discrepancy — Menlo Ventures is a top-tier US VC and its presence on the cap table would be highly significant; this must be confirmed directly via wire or LP documentation before relying on it in any investment memo. this is a strategic bet on proprietary quality rather than a platform-agnostic approach.","ipTimeToReplicate":"The combination of a custom codec optimised for latent learnability, a diffusion transformer trained with proprietary hyperparameter scaling insights, and a purpose-built GAW UX represents roughly 2–3 years of compounding work that would take a well-funded team 12–18 months to replicate from scratch."},"notes":[],"createdAt":1782119919686,"marketStudy":{"problemThesis":"The global audio content production stack is structurally broken for modern B2B buyers: game studios, media companies, advertising agencies, and content platforms still rely on expensive, time-consuming workflows involving human Foley artists, licensed stock libraries, and bespoke composer engagements. A single AAA game title can require tens of thousands of bespoke sound assets, while podcast networks, ad agencies, and streaming platforms face relentless pressure to produce localized, royalty-clean audio at scale and at speed. Legacy royalty-free libraries (Epidemic Sound, Artlist) are static and non-generative — they cannot adapt to context, brand, or real-time interaction. Concurrently, the broader AI audio market is expanding at a 27–30% CAGR, with the AI music and sound generation segment projected to reach $21.8B by 2034, driven by gaming, media, and advertising adoption. Copyright litigation risk (e.g., RIAA suits against Suno and Udio in 2024) has created a clear market gap for a commercially licensed, enterprise-grade generative audio API. The convergence of rapidly improving transformer-based audio models, enterprise demand for programmatic audio infrastructure, and legal resolution pressure from the content industry creates a decisive 2025–2027 window for a well-positioned generative audio platform to establish category leadership.","companyOverview":"Audiogen is an AI-native generative audio platform offering a B2B API and SaaS interface for on-demand creation of sound effects, music beds, ambient textures, and voice-compatible audio assets from natural language text prompts. Operating at Seed stage across the US and EU, Audiogen's technical wedge lies in its proprietary audio foundation model — an autoregressive transformer architecture trained on commercially licensed audio datasets, enabling royalty-free output from day one and bypassing the copyright liability that has plagued consumer-facing competitors. The platform integrates directly into game engines (Unreal, Unity), video editing suites (Adobe Premiere Pro, DaVinci Resolve), and custom API pipelines via REST/WebSocket interfaces, targeting audio leads and technical directors at studios, publishers, and content agencies. Unlike consumer-facing tools such as Suno or Udio (which lack enterprise APIs and carry copyright risk), Audiogen positions as the 'ElevenLabs for sound effects and music beds' — a developer-first, commercially safe, API-first infrastructure layer for the $220B gaming and $131B podcasting ecosystems.","willingnessToPay":"• Cost displacement: A single licensed stock SFX library subscription (e.g., Boom Library, Pro Sound Effects) runs $500–$5,000/year per studio seat, while bespoke Foley and composer work for a mid-tier game title can exceed $200,000–$500,000 per project — generative AI reduces this by 60–80%.\n• Production velocity: AI-generated audio eliminates 40–60% of audio production cycle time, directly compressing go-to-market timelines for game studios and ad agencies operating on tight release schedules.\n• Royalty-clean commercial licensing: Enterprise buyers — especially ad agencies and media companies — face real legal exposure from AI audio with ambiguous IP terms; a clean commercial license is a procurement prerequisite worth a meaningful price premium.\n• API-first developer integration: Studios building procedural or adaptive audio (e.g., game soundscapes that react to player state) cannot achieve this with static libraries — generative API access unlocks entirely new product features with high retention.\n• Localization at scale: Streaming platforms and global brands needing multilingual audio assets at volume face prohibitive costs with human production; AI generation reduces per-asset cost by 90%+.\n• Labor scarcity and budget pressure: Nearly one-third of artists now use AI for mastering, and 84% of business leaders plan to increase spending on voice/audio AI technology — signaling broad institutional WTP for solutions that replace or augment scarce audio talent.","businessModel":{"pricingStructure":"Hybrid SaaS + consumption model: tiered annual platform license (Starter ~$6K/yr, Professional ~$18K/yr, Enterprise ~$48K+/yr) covering a seat allocation and API call allowance, with overage charges per generation credit above plan thresholds. SAM of ~38,000 reflects the English-language US/EU addressable subset with (a) digital-first workflows, (b) existing SaaS tooling budgets, (c) commercial audio output volume justifying >$5K/yr spend — estimated at ~20% of TAM, consistent with Audiogen's Seed-stage geographic and GTM focus. SOM of ~1,800 units represents a realistic 3–5 year capture target of ~4.7% of SAM, benchmarked against comparable Seed-to-Series A SaaS trajectories in the creative tools vertical (ElevenLabs reached ~1M users within 2 years but B2B enterprise contracts scale more slowly). ScrapChef is not a point tool — it is the emerging operating system for intelligent EAF production, and BNVT wants to be the co-investor that helps Foresight Data Machines own that category."},"slide2":{"title":"The $17B EAF Bottleneck: Why 500+ Electric Steelmakers Are Flying Blind on Scrap, Energy & Yield — and Paying Billions for It","content":"Electric arc furnaces now account for nearly 29% of global steel output and are the fastest-growing production route globally, with over 71% of new steelmaking capacity additions worldwide over the past three years being EAF-based. Yet despite this structural shift, EAF operators have historically relied on operator intuition, static rule-of-thumb recipes, and legacy Level-2 automation systems (from OEMs like Primetals, SMS Group, and Danieli) that were designed for hardware control — not real-time AI-driven material and energy optimization. The operational bottleneck is acute: scrap is the primary cost input (often 60–70% of total production cost), yet scrap composition, pricing, and availability are deeply volatile. Energy represents the second major variable cost, and with the EU ETS carbon price projected to rise from ~€70/tonne in 2025 toward ~€130/tonne by 2030, unoptimized energy consumption is becoming a direct financial liability — not just an efficiency gap. The EU CBAM entered its definitive enforcement phase on January 1, 2026, meaning steel importers and producers with high embedded CO2 are now legally required to purchase CBAM certificates, with iron and steel facing the most significant carbon border exposure, accounting for roughly 75% of potential CBAM liabilities. At EUA prices of ~€90 in 2026, importers of high-intensity steel could face extra costs of €40–60 per tonne. For EAF operators, AI-driven scrap optimization and energy reduction are no longer ROI conversations — they are compliance and survivability conversations. The EAF hardware market itself is projected to grow from ~$6.9B in 2024 to $17.5B by 2035 at an 8.7% CAGR. The embedded AI software sub-market — targeting process optimization, scrap management, and quality control — is the high-margin, capital-light overlay layer on top of this hardware buildout, and it remains largely unaddressed by purpose-built, AI-native vendors.","bullets":["TAM Anchor: EAF market estimated at $6.9B (2024) → $17.5B (2035) at 8.7% CAGR; 71%+ of all new global steelmaking capacity additions now EAF-based; 57% of global steel plants plan to upgrade to newer EAF units within the next decade — creating a structural, multi-decade software installation opportunity.","CBAM Forcing Function (Live Jan 2026): EU CBAM financial obligations activated January 1, 2026 — iron and steel bear ~75% of all CBAM liabilities; EUA prices projected to rise from ~€70/t (2025) to ~€130/t (2030), making every tonne of unoptimized scrap or energy a measurable P&L liability and converting ScrapChef from efficiency software into compliance infrastructure.","Incumbent Gap — The OEM Software Blindspot: Legacy Level-2 automation providers (Primetals, SMS Group, Danieli) control the hardware layer but deliver static, recipe-based process models that cannot dynamically optimize multi-variable scrap purchasing, real-time energy arbitrage, and yield quality simultaneously — the exact gap Foresight Data Machines is purpose-built to fill with its physics + ML hybrid architecture."]},"slide3":{"title":"Physics + ML Hybrid Architecture, Forward-Deployed Engineers, and a Seed Syndicate That Signals Category Credibility: Why Foresight Data Machines Is Built to Win EAF Process Control","content":"Foresight Data Machines has assembled a defensible technical and commercial foundation that is rare at the seed stage in industrial AI. The company's core thesis — pairing AI with physics to solve the most important control problems in electric steelmaking — is the correct architectural bet for a domain where pure data-driven ML fails due to sparse, noisy, and non-stationary industrial sensor data. Physics-based constraints (thermodynamic models of the EAF heat process, metallurgical reaction kinetics) provide the model guardrails that allow ML to generalize reliably across different scrap grades, furnace configurations, and operating conditions. This hybrid approach is more defensible than pure ML competitors because it encodes deep domain expertise that cannot be replicated by horizontal industrial AI platforms. None of these platforms natively understand scrap grade economics, EAF heat chemistry, or the multi-objective trade-off between energy cost, yield, and steel quality that defines the EAF operator's daily decision space. Their AI additions (e.g., Primetals' AI-enabled arc stabilization) are narrow, single-function features bolted onto legacy systems, not integrated optimization platforms. The closest private-market analog — Smart Steel Technologies (SST), which builds production planning and scheduling software for steel — competes primarily on logistics/scheduling rather than real-time scrap purchasing and EAF process control. AI-driven EAF optimization companies that establish deep customer relationships and proprietary process data sets today will be extraordinarily difficult to displace — because their models compound in accuracy over time as more heat data flows through them. Extracted text:\n\n--- Page 1 ---\n\n--- Page 2 ---\n\n--- Page 3 ---\n\n--- Page 4 ---\n\n--- Page 5 ---\n\n--- Page 6 ---\n\n--- Page 7 ---\n\n--- Page 8 ---\n\n--- Page 9 ---\n\n--- Page 10 ---\n\n--- Page 11 ---\n\n--- Page 12 ---\n\n--- Page 13 ---\n\n--- Page 14 ---\n\n--- Page 15 ---\n\n--- Page 16 ---\n\n--- Page 17 ---\n\n--- Page 18 ---\n\n--- Page 19 ---\n\n--- Page 20 ---\n\n--- Page 21 ---\n\n--- Page 22 ---\n\n--- Page 23 ---\n\n--- Page 24 ---\n\n--- Page 25 ---\n\n--- Page 26 ---\n\n--- Page 27 ---\n\n--- Page 28 ---\n\n--- Page 29 ---\n\n--- Page 30 ---\n\n","via":"pdf"}],"createdAt":1781721180845,"diligenceMemo":{"problemThesis":"Enterprises today are trapped in a structural data paradox: they have never generated more data, yet they have never been less able to act on it efficiently. According to the 2025 State of Analytics Engineering Report, 80% of data professionals now use AI daily — up from 30% the prior year — yet 57% still spend most of their time maintaining or organising data sets rather than driving business decisions. This is not a tooling gap; it is an architectural gap. Legacy BI incumbents (Tableau, Looker, Power BI) were built for a dashboard-first, analyst-gated world. They require SQL literacy, dedicated data engineering headcount, weeks-long implementation cycles, and produce static artefacts that go stale the moment data moves. Survey data from Dataversity's 2025 TDM study compounds the case: 62% of data professionals report incomplete data, 58% cite capture inconsistencies, and a damning 75% of business leaders admit they do not trust their data for decision-making. Meanwhile, Gartner warns that data leaders are failing to scale AI projects due to poor data quality, and IDC projects a 15% productivity loss by 2027 for organisations that fail to achieve AI-ready data. The enterprise data stack in 2025 is characterised by point-solution sprawl — a patchwork of cloud warehouses, separate data lakes, and siloed ML systems that creates high costs, governance gaps, and compounding engineering overhead. The agentic AI layer is the logical architectural resolution: a governance-first intelligence tier that sits above all existing infrastructure, ingests 300+ heterogeneous data sources natively, performs its own extraction, transformation, and contextualisation, and then exposes a single governed source of truth accessible via natural language to every business role — no SQL, no tickets, no stale dashboards. Agentic architectures are not simply AI-assisted dashboards; they are autonomous multi-step reasoning systems that can proactively surface non-obvious insights, trigger workflows, monitor anomalies, and produce predictive outputs without human prompting. Alation's 2026 data management trends analysis confirms: by end-2026, agentic AI should produce visible reductions in manual data effort, with far fewer match/merge decisions requiring human review. Oraion's architectural thesis — VPC deployment in under five days, zero disruption to existing infrastructure, Slack/Teams-native interaction, and role-specific dashboards across sales, finance, marketing, and operations — maps precisely onto this unmet enterprise need, positioning the company as the governance-first, agentic intelligence layer rather than yet another visualisation front-end.","marketSizingCheck":"Oraion's stated market context is well-supported by multiple independent research houses. On the enterprise AI TAM, Research Nester pegs the global enterprise AI market at USD 30.18 billion in 2025, projecting USD 570.36 billion by 2035 at a CAGR of 34.20% — numbers that align precisely with the company's cited figures and carry independent third-party validation. Grand View Research, which takes a slightly broader lens, values enterprise AI at USD 23.95 billion in 2024 and projects USD 155.21 billion by 2030 at a 37.6% CAGR, suggesting the 2025 base figure of ~$30B is methodologically defensible across multiple segmentation approaches. On the AI-in-data-analytics adjacent market, Precedence Research is the most closely aligned source, computing the global AI in data analytics market at USD 31.22 billion in 2025 and projecting USD 310.97 billion by 2034 at a 29.10% CAGR — again directionally consistent with the company's cited $311B / 29% CAGR figure. Market Research Future values the broader data analytics market at USD 89.7 billion in 2024, growing at 27.6% CAGR through 2035, providing a wider addressable ceiling. The critical caveat in assessing Oraion's TAM narrative is definitional: the company's $570B enterprise AI TAM is the entire enterprise AI market, not the agentic data intelligence sub-segment alone. Oraion competes primarily in the intersection of agentic analytics, natural language BI, and governed data integration — a sub-segment BNVT Capital estimates at $15-25B in 2025 based on carving AI-native analytics software from the broader BI/analytics spend and applying the Precedence CAGR. The North American market, where Oraion is targeting primary expansion, accounts for approximately 35-37% of the global enterprise AI market (Grand View Research: 35.5% in 2024; Research Nester: 37% projected for 2035), which provides a credible geographic SAM anchor. Supply-side validation also exists: enterprise generative AI spend reached USD 37 billion in 2025, up from USD 11.5 billion the prior year, confirming that budgets are already flowing into this category. The company's TAM framing is directionally accurate but requires sharpening at the pitch layer — analysts will flag that the $570B figure conflates Oraion's serviceable universe with the total enterprise AI spend, and a bottom-up SAM calculation anchored in addressable enterprise counts, ACV assumptions, and penetration rates will be essential before Series A.","isPainReal":"The pain that Oraion targets is extensively validated by independent benchmarking and industry research.
